target.title: feat(msteams): Microsoft Teams voice (CVI) + video + chat + governance
target.body:
## Summary Adds **Microsoft Teams as a first-class voice, video, and chat channel** for OpenClaw via the `msteams` provider and a full Conversational Video Interface (CVI). The agent answers and places Teams calls, *sees* the caller's camera and screen-share, *converses* in real time, *appears* as a lip-synced animated avatar, participates in group meetings (speaking only when addressed), and handles Teams chat — all with recording-gated, allowlisted, HMAC-authenticated transport. Teams audio/video cannot reach OpenClaw's existing webhook/media plane: Teams calls are delivered through Graph Calling signalling + the `Microsoft.Skype.Bots.Media` socket, which only run on .NET/Windows. This PR adds the OpenClaw-side orchestrator and the additive bridge protocol; a **separate companion media worker** (its own repo) owns the Graph/Skype.Bots.Media socket and renders the avatar tile. The three CVI pillars map directly onto what lands here: | Pillar | Meaning | In this PR | | --- | --- | --- | | **Perception** | the agent *sees* | inbound `video.frame` vision — `look_at_screen` + streaming auto-attach + realtime continuous vision (`sendImage`); camera + screen-share (VBSS); per-participant attribution in meetings | | **Dialogue** | the agent *converses* | realtime speech-to-speech (+ streaming STT→agent→TTS), barge-in, deterministic verbal interrupts, group "speak only when addressed" gate, recording-gated | | **Rendering** | the agent *is seen* | a lip-synced avatar tile driven by this PR's **expression cues**, **viseme `speech.marks`**, and **`show_to_caller`/`display.image`** senders (rendered by the companion worker) | ## What's included **Voice** - `msteams` provider on both voice-call modes — **streaming** (STT → agent → TTS) and **realtime** (speech-to-speech), with barge-in. - Caller **allowlist**, closed by default; recording-status gate before any media-derived data is processed. - **Realtime delegation** — the realtime model handles small talk and delegates real work to the OpenClaw agent: `openclaw_agent_consult` for inline answers (a "working on it" filler covers longer runs) and, under `realtime.toolPolicy: "owner"`, `openclaw_agent_task` for background jobs. - **Deterministic verbal interrupts** — "stop" / "wait" / "hold on" / "never mind" handled in code (streaming cuts playback and suppresses the reply; realtime flushes the worker queue), with wake-phrase-form and filler stripping, whole-utterance matching, and Unicode-aware Arabic support. - **DTMF / IVR** — in-band keypad tones surfaced to the realtime model so the agent can run "press 1 to…" flows (gated until recording is active). - **Bilingual Arabic/English** (opt-in) — detect, mirror, switch-with-caller, translate on request; the deterministic layers (interrupts, visemes) understand Arabic regardless. - **Roster greeting** — greets the caller by first name from the Teams roster and addresses participants by per-utterance speaker label. **Inbound video vision** - `video.frame` ingestion (buffered per source); `look_at_screen` tool (realtime) + automatic per-turn frame attachment (streaming); the agent sees the caller's **camera** and **screen-share**. - **Realtime continuous vision** — `RealtimeVoiceBridge.sendImage` (a small core plugin-sdk addition + openai/azure realtime provider) pushes the latest changed frame as an ambient `input_image` item every ~6 s, no forced response, budget-capped and recording-gated. - **Scene-change-driven ambient vision** (camera + screen simultaneously, per-source dedup + labels) and **retroactive vision** — a ring of 16 scene-change keyframes per call; `look_at_screen scope:"history"` attaches up to 6 timestamped, attributed keyframes to one budgeted consult. - Per-call **vision spend cap** (`maxVisionPerMinute`, default 30, `0` = unlimited) across all consumers. **CVI rendering drivers** (sent by this PR; rendered by the companion worker; additive — older workers ignore unknown fields) - **Expression cues** — a dependency-free lexical heuristic infers neutral/happy/sad/surprised from reply text and emits an `expression` message; a "thinking" expression holds while a tool runs. - **Viseme lip-sync** — `speech.marks` carry a per-sound timeline so the avatar mouth changes shape; the full pass uses real ElevenLabs `/with-timestamps` character alignment (falls back to a text estimator when absent; audio never depends on timing). - **Visual sharing** — `show_to_caller` runs the agent to produce one trusted-local image (remote URLs excluded — no SSRF) and renders it via `display.image`, either fullscreen or as a **picture-in-picture overlay** over the avatar, with caption strips and paced multi-image slideshows. **Group / meeting calls** - **Per-participant attribution** — each frame carries `participantId` / `participantName`; the streaming path labels the attached image and `look_at_screen` names the participant, so the model can reason about who is showing what. Active-speaker camera follow / re-select. - **Speak only when addressed** — in a group call (2+ humans) the assistant stays silent until addressed by name (`wakePhrases`), with a short follow-up window; 1:1 always responds. Deterministic on streaming (`shouldRespondToGroupTurn`, evaluated at the audio egress on realtime too); time-windowed and partial-wake aware. **Outbound (call me back)** - `initiateCall` places a Teams call via the authenticated, SSRF-guarded endpoint; the agent speaks the **result**, then hangs up. Delivery via Teams **chat message** (default) or **voice call-back**. - **Greet-on-answer** — the call-back speaks only once the callee answers (recording-active), not while the phone is still ringing. - Notify mode delivers the result and hangs up drain-aware (no clipping); voicemail fallback leaves the same result when unanswered; no-answer timeout finalizes calls that never connect. **Chat & governance** - **"Ask OpenClaw about this" message action** — a Teams message-context command quotes the selected chat message into a prompt and dispatches it through the normal message path (requires the app manifest to declare the `askOpenClaw` command). - **Voice-message transcription** (opt-in) — inbound Teams audio clips are transcribed and folded into the agent's turn; a clip that fails keeps its attachment placeholder. - **Audit-log channel** (opt-in) — agent replies are mirrored to a named Teams conversation at the delivery choke point (so natively-streamed replies are in the trail too); a loop guard keeps the audit channel's own traffic out. - **DLP outbound redaction** (opt-in) — built-in categories plus custom patterns redacted on every outbound surface (block messages, streamed replies, edits, adaptive cards, poll cards). Because per-token streaming can't retract a secret completing across chunks, DLP downgrades `streaming.mode: "partial"` to `"progress"`. **Meeting productivity** - **End-of-meeting recap** (opt-in) — key points / decisions / action items posted to the caller's Teams chat (1:1) or into the meeting thread (group) at call end. - **On-demand minutes** mid-call (`post_meeting_minutes`), a real **OOXML `.docx` minutes artifact** (built deterministically in code via `jszip`), and **per-person attribution** from unmixed meeting audio ("Sara: …" instead of one mixed stream) rendered into the document. **Sessions & memory** - Caller identity (Teams AAD object id, else a per-call-unique id) keys the session; `sessionScope: "per-thread"` keys by Teams meeting/chat thread so parallel conversations stay isolated. PSTN falls through to per-phone. ## Architecture OpenClaw runs the orchestration in this PR — the `msteams` voice-call provider, the HMAC-authenticated WebSocket bridge, STT/agent/TTS and realtime delegation, the group/vision/governance logic, and all CVI **drivers** (`expression`, `speech.marks`, `display.image`, `sendImage`). A **separate companion Windows/.NET media worker** (its own repo) owns the Graph Calling signalling and the `Microsoft.Skype.Bots.Media` socket, and renders the avatar tile (RMS lip-sync blended with viseme shapes, head bob/sway, blink, gaze, expression-shaped mouths, and the `display.image` overlay). It pairs with this provider over the versioned, additive bridge protocol. The worker is intentionally **out of scope here** — this PR adds no Teams/Graph SDK and no new runtime dependencies (reuses `ws` / `zod` / plugin-sdk), so msteams logic can be bundled in `voice-call` or lifted into a separate optional plugin per maintainer preference. ## Recent reliability fixes - **Echo self-answer fix** — `realtime.suppressInputDuringPlayback` now defaults **on** (default-off was a live self-answer bug); the echo guard runs on a playout clock, and the opening greeting allows no barge-in until the caller's first real turn, so a speakerphone echo can't make the bot re-greet in a loop. - **Group-meeting wake gate** — time-windowed, partial-wake; an unaddressed meeting turn's reply audio is dropped deterministically at the egress on the realtime path (not model-trusted). - **Meeting-minutes recipient** — recap/minutes are target-locked to the caller's chat (1:1) or the meeting thread (group), gated behind the opt-in so the bot never posts unprompted. - **Screen-share** — `look_at_screen` offered under a safe read-only posture with browser auto-start guidance; scene-change detection so switching shared content is picked up, not stuck on the previous page. - **Replay-proof handshake** — single-use `(callId, timestamp, signature)` HMAC tuples expiring at `ts+window` (clock-skew safe); legitimate reconnects carry a fresh timestamp. - **Teardown** — a single `releaseCallState` owner every path funnels through (no stranded "un-hangup-able" calls, no leaked vision/recording state, declined outbound calls release their CallRecord). ## Behavior changes Defaults preserve prior behavior except two flagged changes: - `realtime.suppressInputDuringPlayback` flipped **off → on** (fixes the self-answer echo bug; set `false` to restore, tune via `echoSuppressionWindowMs` / `echoBargeInRms`). - Outbound call-backs honor `outbound.defaultMode` (default **notify**: state the result, then hang up after audio drains) and **speak only on answer** (set `"conversation"` to keep the lingering behavior). All other additions are opt-in: `msteams.meetingRecap`, `msteams.bilingual`, `channels.msteams.transcribeVoiceMessages`, `channels.msteams.auditChannel`, `channels.msteams.dlp.enabled`. ## Config (example) ```jsonc "voice-call": { "config": { "msteams": { "port": 8443, "path": "/voice/msteams/stream", "sharedSecret": "", "requireRecordingStatus": true, "bindAddress": "127.0.0.1", "maxVisionPerMinute": 30, "outbound": { "enabled": true, "workerBaseUrl": "", "answerTimeoutMs": 120000 }, "groupCall": { "requireAddress": true, "wakePhrases": ["assistant", ""], "followUpWindowMs": 12000 } }, "realtime": { "enabled": true } } } ``` `tenantId` is read from `channels.msteams.tenantId`. `groupCall` only affects calls with 2+ humans; 1:1 always responds. `maxVisionPerMinute` caps per-call vision spend across `look_at_screen`, streaming auto-attach, and the realtime ambient push. Audio is **PCM 16 kHz**. ### Security - WS server binds **loopback by default** (`bindAddress` for a trusted interface); HMAC-SHA256 over `timestamp + callId`, constant-time compare; replay window; 64 KB inbound payload cap; per-IP / total / pending connection caps; strict `zod` discriminated-union message validation; secret as `SecretInput`/SecretRef, never logged; SSRF-guarded outbound place-call. Lifecycle-managed: the server starts as an awaited init step (bind failures surface) and stops on teardown. ## Relationship to other PRs Coexists with the foundation PR **#91438** (Microsoft Teams CVI voice/video provider); this PR is not stacked on it. ## Testing / proof Each feature ships unit tests — provider lifecycle, WS transport (HMAC accept/reject, replay 401/reconnect, payload-cap close `1009`), the pure group-call gate, interrupt matcher (positive/negative, wake-phrase, Arabic), viseme/display paths, history ring, group-gate egress drop, recap prompt, DTMF mapping, message-action extraction, teardown/leak regressions, and pre-recording transcript drop. Core flows were verified end-to-end on **real Microsoft Teams calls** (production M365 tenant; gateway from source; companion media worker as a Windows service; Azure OpenAI realtime; ElevenLabs STT/TTS), across 1:1 and group calls, voice and CVI: - **Voice / realtime** — multi-turn live ASR→agent→TTS round-trips; roster greeting by name; no echo self-answer on open speakers; barge-in; deterministic interrupts in English, wake-phrase form, and Arabic ("توقف"); bilingual mirroring + on-request translation. - **Vision / CVI** — `look_at_screen` answered across multiple screen-share windows (incl. content switches and "what did the first screen say"); the avatar published a live video tile; the "thinking" expression held across tool runs; `show_to_caller` rendered captioned PiP slideshows with the coded hold times; real ElevenLabs `(aligned)` viseme timing observed. - **Outbound** — call-back placed → Established → recording-active gate → result spoken only after answer → notify-mode auto-hangup after drain → clean teardown, no leaked state; voicemail fallback delivered the same result when unanswered. - **Meeting / chat / governance** — on-hangup recap + minutes landed in the Teams chat; background task delivered its answer to chat; audit mirror produced one line per streamed DM reply with no self-mirror inside the audit channel; a DLP-redacted email rendered as `[REDACTED:email]` on the streamed wire path; the "Ask OpenClaw about this" message action dispatched and replied. Not live-tested in this tenant (unit-tested instead): voice-message transcription (the tenant exposes no bot-deliverable voice clip; the graceful no-media path was observed), the DLP `secret` category on the wire (the model refused to repeat a dummy key; the email category exercises the same path), and the group-chat form of the message action. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Microsoft Teams voice/video/CVI + chat as a first-class OpenClaw channel — the `msteams` voice provider answers/places real Teams calls, sees the caller's screen-share/camera, renders a lip-synced avatar, and runs chat governance (DLP / audit / minutes). - **Real environment tested:** Production Microsoft 365 tenant; OpenClaw gateway built from source; the companion Windows/.NET media worker running as a Windows service; Azure OpenAI realtime; ElevenLabs STT/TTS. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Enabled `voice-call` `provider: "msteams"` + `realtime` and the `channels.msteams` governance blocks in `openclaw.json`, started the gateway (`openclaw gateway run --force`), then placed real Teams 1:1 and group calls (voice + screen-share), an outbound "call me back", and a chat-governance session. - **Evidence after fix (screenshots, recording, redacted logs):** Captured from real Microsoft Teams calls against a production tenant (redacted): **1. Be-seen avatar — the bot publishes its avatar as a live video tile** **2. `show_to_caller` — the bot screenshots a page and displays it fullscreen on its own video tile mid-call** **3. Outbound "call me back" — the bot rings the caller on Teams** **4. Result delivery even to voicemail — Teams transcribes the call-back's spoken result** **5. Screen recording of a live call** https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a961d8ce-22f8-44a9-ae28-b475393e3115 Plus the redacted gateway/worker logs + transcripts in the **Proof & live-test evidence** section below (voice ASR→agent→TTS round-trip, realtime session lifecycle, the three-call recording-gate refuse→open, outbound notify call-back lifecycle, background-task→Teams-chat delivery reading the real machine clock, audit `🧾` mirror, and DLP `[REDACTED:email]` on the streamed wire path). - **Observed result after fix:** Multi-turn round-trips with audible replies; `look_at_screen` answered across two screen-share windows incl. a content switch + retroactive history; the avatar published a live video tile; the outbound call-back spoke the result only after answer then auto-hung-up cleanly with no leaked state; the on-hangup recap + minutes landed in the caller's Teams chat as a real `.docx`; DLP redacted a secret on the live streamed reply. - **What was not tested:** Voice-message transcription (the tenant exposed no bot-deliverable voice clip; the graceful no-media path was observed), the DLP `secret` category on the wire (the model refused to repeat a dummy key; the `email` category exercises the same path), and the group-chat form of the message action — each is unit-tested instead. - **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Private tenant identifiers (AAD object ids, call ids, endpoints) are redacted, and some evidence is redacted runtime logs rather than recordings; the avatar is rendered by the companion media worker (separate repo), so its screenshot is the viewable artifact for the CVI rendering drivers this PR emits. - **Before evidence (optional but encouraged):** The pre-fix `realtime.suppressInputDuringPlayback` default-off self-answer bug was reproduced before the fix (a speakerphone echo made the bot re-greet itself in a loop); fixed by defaulting it on plus the playout-clock echo guard. ## Proof & live-test evidence All evidence below is from real Microsoft Teams calls against a production tenant (private identifiers redacted). ![OpenClaw companion worker rendering the animated "jarvis" avatar live on the bot's Teams video tile during a production-tenant validation call](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/06af3f1f-b943-4a40-88c9-cd4eb95fc7ba) *Avatar shown is the worker-side `AvatarImagePath=jarvis` Lottie loop with speech-reactive zoom (consuming this PR's `expression` / `speech.marks` viseme / `display.image` rendering drivers); the default `AvatarImagePath=avatar` SVG style adds full lip-sync viseme mouth shapes, expressions, and blink.* ### Voice / realtime **Full inbound audio round-trip on a real multi-turn Teams call** — caller speech → ElevenLabs STT → agent (`microsoft-foundry/gpt-5.4`) → ElevenLabs TTS, across multiple turns (~70 s). Disfluencies in the transcript confirm live ASR; `spoken` payloads are exactly what was synthesized back to the caller. ``` user (STT): "How are you, man?" asst (spoken): "I'm doing well, man - thanks. What can I help you with?" user (STT): "Tell me more about you." asst (spoken): "I'm your phone assistant in OpenClaw - calm, practical, here to help..." ```
Gateway lifecycle log (streaming path, provider msteams) ``` MsteamsMediaStream listening port=8443 path=/voice/msteams/stream [voice-call] msteams realtime transcription provider: elevenlabs [voice-call] msteams TTS provider configured MsteamsMediaStream: connection open MsteamsProvider: session.start callId= threadId= caller.aadId= MsteamsMediaStream: connection closed ```
**Speech-to-speech realtime mode on a real call** — operator placed a Teams call, spoke several turns, and heard the OpenAI-realtime bot reply; clean session lifecycle, 0 errors. **Verbal interrupts / barge-in on the ~7.5 min inbound 1:1 validation call** — deterministic verbal interrupts in English, by wake-phrase, and in Arabic ("توقف"); bilingual mirroring + translation; consult with the "thinking" avatar expression across six tool runs. ### Vision & CVI **`look_at_screen` and `show_to_caller` on the same validation call** — `look_at_screen` handled a scene change plus retroactive keyframe history across two screen-share windows; `show_to_caller` rendered a captioned PiP slideshow at exactly the coded hold times (4500 ms non-final / 5000 ms final), verified in the worker log. The avatar screenshot above is the live consumer of the `expression` / `speech.marks` viseme / `display.image` drivers this PR emits. ### Outbound call-back **Outbound "call me when it's done" notify call-back on a real call**, placed by an in-call background task: `placed → Established → recording-gate open → result spoken only after answer → notify-mode auto-hangup after audio drain` — complete, un-clipped delivery with clean teardown. **`openclaw_agent_task` background task → Teams chat delivery on a real call.** Caller said *"take care of local time in the background and ping me on Teams."* The realtime model invoked `openclaw_agent_task`, spoke the ack, the caller hung up, the agent ran in the background, and the result landed in the caller's Teams chat — reading the real machine clock, not a canned reply: ``` > I'm checking the local machine time now and will send it here shortly. > Local machine time: 2026-06-04 05:28:13 +00:00. ``` ### Meeting / chat / governance **Recording-status gate (Microsoft Media Access API) observed refusing, then opening, across real calls.** With `requireRecordingStatus: true` (default), delegation is refused until the external worker signals recording active (the caller audibly hears a "recording isn't active" reply), then the gate opens and `openclaw_agent_consult` runs.
Redacted gateway log across real calls (recording gate) ``` # Call A — no recording signal → consult refused MsteamsProvider: realtime session.start callId=0c005180… caller.aadId= MsteamsMediaStream: connection closed 0c005180… # Call B — worker reports recording active → gate opens → consult runs MsteamsProvider: realtime session.start callId=2d005480… caller.aadId= MsteamsProvider: recording.status 2d005480… = active (realtime) <-- gate opens ```
**Governance on the wire** — the audit-channel mirror produced a `🧾` line per streamed DM reply (loop guard holding for the bot's own reply inside the audit chat), and DLP rendered `[REDACTED:email]` in the Teams client on the **streamed** path. On-hangup recap plus on-demand minutes both landed in chat. ### Message action — "Ask OpenClaw about this" The Teams message-context command quotes the selected chat message into a prompt and dispatches it through the normal message path; on the 2026-06-12 governance session it **dispatched and replied** in the Teams client. The invoke-dispatch path was merged with upstream's adaptive-card value helpers in `monitor-handler.ts` (both kept), with message-action extraction unit-covered. (The group-chat form is unit-tested, not live-tested in this tenant.) ### Setup / upgrade compatibility (fresh-start & upgrade path) **Everything here is additive and backward-compatible — no data-model migration is required** (this is the upgrade-path evidence for the compatibility / "serialized state" flags): - **All new behavior is OPT-IN config.** The `msteams` voice provider and every governance feature are off unless explicitly configured: `msteams.meetingRecap`, `msteams.bilingual`, `channels.msteams.transcribeVoiceMessages`, `channels.msteams.auditChannel`, `channels.msteams.dlp.enabled`, plus the `msteams.groupCall` / `msteams.outbound` blocks. A config that doesn't set these behaves exactly as before. - **Existing voice-call providers are unaffected.** `twilio` / `telnyx` / `plivo` / `mock` route and behave unchanged; `msteams` is a new enum case resolved only when selected, on its own authenticated bridge listener (`msteams.port`/`path`), not the public webhook plane. - **plugin-sdk additions are optional with a no-op facade.** `RealtimeVoiceImageInput` / `sendImage` and the TTS alignment surface are optional contract additions; realtime providers that don't implement them get a no-op facade — no behavior change for non-Teams voice. - **No persisted-state migration.** The files flagged as "serialized state" are **test fixtures** (`elevenlabs/*.test.ts`, `openai/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts`) and **in-memory per-call state** (`msteams-realtime.ts`, `session-runtime.ts`); nothing persisted crosses versions. `bundled-channel-config-metadata.generated.ts` is **regenerated tooling output** (the validation schema for the new opt-in keys), not user data — old configs validate as before. - **Two flagged default changes only**, both fixing live-reproduced bugs and both reversible: `realtime.suppressInputDuringPlayback` off → on (self-answer echo fix; set `false` to restore), and outbound `defaultMode` defaults to `notify` + speak-only-on-answer (set `"conversation"` to restore). **New real `.docx` minutes.** End-of-meeting minutes are now a real OOXML Word document with **deterministic per-speaker attribution** ("Sara: …") from unmixed meeting audio, delivered to chat alongside the on-hangup recap. Unit coverage: the voice-call suite is **610 passing**, with dedicated suites for the docx minutes, welcome-card DLP, recording gate, interrupt matcher (incl. Arabic), and DLP cards. ## Acknowledgements Contributed by the team at the **Ports, Customs and Free Zone Corporation (PCFC)** — Dubai, United Arab Emirates — bringing Microsoft Teams voice & video (the Conversational Video Interface) to OpenClaw for public-sector use. We welcome the maintainers' guidance on shaping it for the wider community.

target.title: fix(discord): reserve closing-fence space on fence-closing lines
target.body:
## Summary

`chunkDiscordText` (`extensions/discord/src/chunk.ts`) could emit a chunk **longer than `maxChars`**, which Discord hard-rejects (HTTP 400) — so the whole message fails to send. The chunker keeps fenced code blocks balanced by reserving room for a closing fence, but it reserved using the **post-line** fence state:

```ts
const reserveChars = nextOpenFence ? closeFenceLine(nextOpenFence).length + 1 : 0;
```

The loop's `flush()` appends the closing fence based on the loop variable `openFence`, which is advanced to `nextOpenFence` only **after** the line's segment loop. So on a line that **closes** a fence but also carries trailing text (e.g. `` ``` `` followed by a long tail, which `parseFenceLine` treats as a closing fence with an info string), `reserveChars` was `0`, yet a mid-line `flush()` still appended `` \n``` `` because `openFence` was still open — producing a chunk of `maxChars + 4` (e.g. **2004 > 2000**).

### Changes
- `chunk.ts` — reserve against `nextOpenFence ?? openFence` (whichever fence a flush can actually close: the still-open `openFence` for a mid-line flush, `nextOpenFence` once the line settles), with an inline comment.
- `chunk.test.ts` — regression test sweeping the boundary (tail lengths 1990–2000) and asserting every chunk stays `<= maxChars`.

`openFence` is null exactly when not inside a fence, so `nextOpenFence ?? openFence` is `nextOpenFence` when still/newly inside, the just-closed fence on a closing line, else null.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: a Discord reply whose text contained a fence-closing line with a long trailing tail produced an over-2000-char chunk that Discord rejects (HTTP 400), dropping the message. After the fix every chunk stays within `maxChars`.

Real environment tested: Node 22.22.1, macOS, no model / network / Discord. A `./node_modules/.bin/tsx` harness imports the real `chunkDiscordText` and (a) chunks the repro and (b) sweeps tail lengths around the boundary, checking the max chunk length. BEFORE is `origin/main`'s `chunk.ts` (swapped in via `git show`, no other change).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
./node_modules/.bin/tsx _discord-proof.mts
```

Evidence after fix:
```
input: "hi\n```lang\n``` + z*1995"  at maxChars 2000

BEFORE (origin/main):
  repro: 2 chunks, lengths=[2004,16], over2000=1
  sweep pad 1980..2010: 24 inputs produced an over-2000 chunk
AFTER (this branch):
  repro: 2 chunks, lengths=[2000,20], over2000=0
  sweep pad 1980..2010: 0 inputs produced an over-2000 chunk
```

Observed result after fix: no chunk exceeds `maxChars` for the repro or anywhere across the boundary sweep; the 16 existing chunk tests are unchanged.

What was not tested: a live send to Discord (the harness drives the real `chunkDiscordText`, which is exactly what the Discord sender calls to split a reply before posting). The new regression test fails on `origin/main` and passes after this change.

## Additional checks
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/discord/src/chunk.test.ts` — all pass with the fix; verified fail-before by swapping in `origin/main`'s `chunk.ts` → the new boundary-sweep test fails (over-limit chunk), the 16 existing tests still pass.
- `oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json` and `tsgo:extensions` pass. Diff: +6/−2 prod, +11 test.
- Note: unrelated CI lanes that run the full suite (e.g. `build-artifacts`) currently fail on `src/channels/progress-draft-compositor.test.ts`, a pre-existing failure on `main` (commit `8a75c4dd5f`) untouched by this Discord-only change; I'll rebase once `main` is green.

### Scope / out of scope

This fixes the **closing-fence-tail** overflow specifically. The `reserveChars` math is unique to the Discord chunker (the qqbot and embedded-agent chunkers use different structures), so no sibling is left half-fixed. Two *other*, **pre-existing** ways a chunk can exceed `maxChars` are present on `main` already and are out of scope here (separate mechanisms, not the reserve logic):
- an **opening fence line longer than `maxChars`** (a very long info-string/language tag): `openFence.openLine` stores the whole oversized line and `flush()` re-prepends it on each reopen.
- `rebalanceReasoningItalics` appends `_` markers to already-emitted chunks with no `maxChars` accounting, so a chunk landing exactly at the limit can be pushed 1–2 over.

Both are worth separate follow-ups; this PR neither introduces nor worsens them on realistic input (verified: realistic code blocks stay within limit and balanced before and after).

target.title: fix(qqbot): recognize GFM table separators with one or two dashes
target.body:
## Summary

The QQ Bot outbound markdown chunker silently dropped most of a table when its separator used one or two dashes. `isTableSeparatorLine` (`extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.ts:436`) required **3+** dashes per cell:

```ts
return cells.length > 0 && cells.every((cell) => /^:?-{3,}:?$/.test(cell.trim()));
```

A GFM delimiter cell needs only **one or more** dashes, so a perfectly valid table whose separator was `|--|--|` (two dashes) or `|-|-|` (one dash) was **not recognized as a separator**. Tracing the chunker state machine: the `if (this.pendingHeaderLine && isTableSeparatorLine(line))` branch never fires, so the table is never confirmed; each following row then hits the `isTableRowLine(line) && !isTableSeparatorLine(line)` branch and **overwrites `pendingHeaderLine` without flushing the prior one** (the only flush is at end of input). The result is silent data loss: the header, the separator, and every row but the last disappear from the message the user receives.

### Changes
- `markdown-table-chunking.ts` — accept `-+` instead of `-{3,}` in `isTableSeparatorLine`, with an inline comment explaining the GFM rule and the failure it prevents.
- `markdown-table-chunking.test.ts` — regression test asserting a table with a one-dash, two-dash, or colon-aligned separator is preserved intact.

The fix matches the GFM spec (delimiter cell = one or more hyphens, optional colons) and the sibling LINE channel (`extensions/line/src/markdown-to-line.ts:22`, `\|[-:\s|]+\|`). Every existing QQ test separator already uses 3+ dashes, so `-{3,}` → `-+` is byte-identical on them (zero test churn).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: a QQ markdown reply containing a table with a 1- or 2-dash separator lost its header and all rows but the last. After the fix the full table is preserved; 3-dash tables are unchanged.

Real environment tested: Node 22.22.1, macOS, no model / network / channel. A `./node_modules/.bin/tsx` harness imports the real exported `chunkQQBotMarkdownText` with a passthrough base chunker (`t => [t]`) and chunks the same table with a 2-dash, 1-dash, and 3-dash separator. BEFORE is `origin/main`'s regex (swapped in via `git show`, no other change).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# harness imports the real chunkQQBotMarkdownText and prints chunks per separator
./node_modules/.bin/tsx _qq-proof.mts
```

Evidence after fix:
```
input: "结果如下：\n| 名称 | 数量 |\n<SEP>\n| 苹果 | 3 |\n| 香蕉 | 5 |"  (limit 500)

BEFORE (origin/main, -{3,}):
  2-dash |--|--|: chunks=["结果如下：","| 香蕉 | 5 |"]   header(名称)=false firstRow(苹果)=false
  1-dash |-|-|  : chunks=["结果如下：","| 香蕉 | 5 |"]   header(名称)=false firstRow(苹果)=false
  3-dash |---|---| (control): full intact table            header(名称)=true  firstRow(苹果)=true
AFTER (this branch, -+):
  2-dash |--|--|: chunks=["结果如下：","| 名称 | 数量 |\n|--|--|\n| 苹果 | 3 |\n| 香蕉 | 5 |"]  header=true firstRow=true
  1-dash |-|-|  : full intact table   header=true firstRow=true
  3-dash |---|---| (control): unchanged
```

Observed result after fix: the 2-dash and 1-dash tables are preserved in full (header + separator + every row); the 3-dash control is byte-identical before and after. A colon-only cell (`|:|:|`, no dash) is still correctly rejected as a non-separator.

What was not tested: a full live QQ send against the QQ platform (the harness drives the real `chunkQQBotMarkdownText`, which is exactly what the outbound dispatcher calls to split a text reply). The new regression test fails on `origin/main` and passes after this change.

## Additional checks
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.test.ts` — 18 pass with the fix; verified fail-before by swapping in `origin/main`'s regex → the new test fails, all 17 existing tests still pass (no churn).
- `oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json` and `tsgo:extensions` pass. Diff: +4/−1 prod, +9 test.

target.title: fix(device-pairing): guard role normalization against non-string entries
target.body:
## Summary

`normalizeRoleList` in `src/shared/device-pairing-access.ts` called `.trim()` on every `roles[]` entry and the singular `role` **without a `typeof === "string"` guard**. The `roles`/`role` fields are loaded from disk via a blind cast (`coercePairingStateRecord`, `src/infra/pairing-files.ts`), so a malformed/legacy pairing record with a non-string entry — e.g. `roles: [123]` or `role: 5` — threw `TypeError: role.trim is not a function`, crashing `resolvePendingDeviceApprovalState`. `openclaw devices list` calls that per pending request with no surrounding try/catch (`src/cli/devices-cli.runtime.ts`), so the whole table render blows up on one bad record.

The scopes half of the same summary (`normalizeDeviceAuthScopes`) is already guarded, and the merged #90654/#92178 work added the same guard to the sibling `mergeRoles`/`mergeScopes`/`formatAuditList` in `src/infra/device-pairing.ts` — but **missed this path**.

This routes each item through the existing shared non-string-safe normalizer `normalizeUniqueSingleOrTrimmedStringList` (the exact helper `mergeRoles` uses), so non-string entries are dropped instead of crashing while valid roles are still trimmed, deduped, and sorted. Net **−10 LOC**; all three `normalizeRoleList` callers (pending request, approved device, token roles) are covered by the single fix.

### Changes
- `src/shared/device-pairing-access.ts` — route `normalizeRoleList` through the guarded shared helper
- `src/shared/device-pairing-access.test.ts` — regression tests for non-string `roles` / `role` / `token.role`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `normalizeRoleList` crashed (`TypeError: role.trim is not a function`) on a non-string role from a malformed pairing record, taking down `resolvePendingDeviceApprovalState` / `openclaw devices list`; it now drops non-string entries and classifies access normally.

Real environment tested: a direct-call harness importing the real `src/shared/device-pairing-access.ts`, run with the repo's `tsx`, Node 22.22.1, no model / provider / channel credentials and no network (the function is pure). The "before" column reverts only that one source file to `origin/main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# dp-proof.mts (~20 lines) imports resolvePendingDeviceApprovalState from the real source
# and calls it with malformed (non-string) roles/role on the pending and paired paths.
tsx dp-proof.mts                                                                      # AFTER (this branch)
git stash push -- src/shared/device-pairing-access.ts && tsx dp-proof.mts && git stash pop   # BEFORE (origin/main)
```

Evidence after fix:
```
########## AFTER FIX (this branch) ##########
OK     pending roles:[123]         -> {"kind":"new-pairing","requested":{"roles":[],"scopes":["operator.read"]},"approved":null}
OK     pending singular role:5     -> {"kind":"new-pairing","requested":{"roles":[],"scopes":["operator.read"]},"approved":null}
OK     paired (approved) roles:[7] -> {"kind":"role-upgrade","requested":{"roles":["operator"],"scopes":["operator.read"]},"approved":{"roles":[],"scopes":["operator.read"]}}
OK     valid+malformed mixed [123,'operator',null,'  admin  '] + role:5 -> {"kind":"new-pairing","requested":{"roles":["admin","operator"],"scopes":["operator.read"]},"approved":null}

########## BEFORE FIX (origin/main) ##########
THROW  pending roles:[123]         -> TypeError: role.trim is not a function
THROW  pending singular role:5     -> TypeError: item.trim is not a function
THROW  paired (approved) roles:[7] -> TypeError: role.trim is not a function
THROW  valid+malformed mixed ...   -> TypeError: role.trim is not a function
```

Observed result after fix: every non-string role entry that previously threw is now dropped, valid roles are trimmed/deduped/sorted (`["admin","operator"]`), and the approval classification returns normally — no crash on any of the three role paths.

What was not tested: end-to-end `openclaw devices list` with a hand-crafted on-disk pairing file holding a non-string role. The pure-function repro exercises the exact crash; reachability is confirmed by source (the `roles`/`role` fields reach `normalizeRoleList` unvalidated through `coercePairingStateRecord`'s blind cast, and the CLI's `parseDevicePairingList` only does `Array.isArray` checks, never element-type checks).

## Additional checks

Four cases in `device-pairing-access.test.ts` cover non-string `roles[]`, singular `role`, and `token.role` (exercising all three callers), alongside the pre-existing valid-role and revoked-token cases. Formatter, static analysis, and type checks all pass for the changed files. Mirrors the merged #90654/#92178 robustness fix; net −10 source LOC.

target.title: fix(cron): trim trailing whitespace from job object keys
target.body:
Fixes #95407

## Problem

When calling the `cron` tool's `add` or `update` action with a `job` parameter, specific top-level keys (`schedule`, `sessionTarget`, `payload`, `enabled`) can arrive with trailing whitespace appended (e.g. `"schedule "` instead of `"schedule"`). This causes the tool to fail with confusing validation errors:

```
invalid cron.add params: must have required property 'schedule'; at root: unexpected property 'schedule '
```

Only specific top-level keys are affected — `name` and `description` remain intact. Nested keys are unaffected.

The trailing whitespace originates in the model provider's tool-call extraction/serialization pipeline (reported with Qwen 3.5B via llama.cpp OpenAI-compatible API). The cron tool should be resilient to this.

## Fix

Added `trimObjectKeys()` to `canonicalizeCronToolObject()` in `cron-tool-canonicalize.ts` that trims whitespace from all object keys before any schema processing. This is defense-in-depth — regardless of where trailing whitespace originates, keys like `"schedule "` become `"schedule"` before reaching validation.

## Tests

5 new tests covering all edge cases:

| Test | Scenario |
|------|----------|
| `trims trailing whitespace from job object keys` | `add` action: `schedule `, `sessionTarget `, `payload `, `enabled ` → trimmed |
| `trims trailing whitespace from patch object keys via update action` | `update` action: patch keys with trailing spaces trimmed |
| `trims leading whitespace from job object keys` | Keys with leading whitespace also trimmed |
| `trims trailing whitespace from flat-recovered job keys` | Flat-params recovery with clean keys works alongside mangled ones |
| `preserves keys without whitespace when trimming` | Normal keys are unaffected |

All 133 tests pass (128 existing + 5 new):

```
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  133 passed (133)
```

## Checks

- Format: `oxfmt` ✓
- Lint: `oxlint` ✓
- Diff: +18 source, +38 test (56 total)

target.title: fix(agents): recognize snake_case tool_use types in id sanitizer for cross-provider failover (#95623)
target.body:
## Summary

`rewriteAssistantToolCallIds` only checks camelCase tool call types (`toolCall`, `toolUse`, `functionCall`), missing snake_case types (`tool_use`, `tool_call`, `function_call`) that OpenAI-responses produces on cross-provider failover replay. Composite tool call IDs like `call_XXX|fc_YYY` pass through unsanitized, and the pipe character reaches the Anthropic target which rejects with 400, bricking the session.

- Problem: `rewriteAssistantToolCallIds` type guard at `tool-call-id.ts:415` uses hardcoded camelCase checks. `type="tool_use"` matches none → block skipped → composite ID with `|` reaches Anthropic → 400 error → session permanently stuck.
- Solution: Add snake_case variants to `TOOL_CALL_TYPES`, switch type guard to `TOOL_CALL_TYPES.has(type)`, and also handle snake_case tool result ID fields (`tool_call_id`, `tool_use_id`) in `rewriteToolResultIds`.
- What changed: `src/agents/tool-call-id.ts` — `TOOL_CALL_TYPES` expanded (1 line), type guard unified (3 lines), `rewriteToolResultIds` extended (10 lines)

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #95623
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

When cross-provider failover replay occurs (retrying an OpenAI session on an Anthropic target), assistant content blocks from the original provider use snake_case type names (`tool_use`, `tool_call`, `function_call`). The tool call ID sanitizer's `rewriteAssistantToolCallIds` only recognized camelCase type names, causing snake_case blocks to be skipped entirely. This left composite tool call IDs (containing pipe characters like `call_XXX|fc_YYY`) unsanitized. These IDs reach the Anthropic API which rejects them with a 400 error, permanently bricking the session.

The `TOOL_CALL_TYPES` set is also used by `extractToolCallsFromAssistant` and `isReplaySafeThinkingAssistantMessage`, which would similarly fail to recognize snake_case blocks. Fixing the set fixes all three consumers at once.

Additionally, `rewriteToolResultIds` only read/wrote camelCase fields (`toolCallId`, `toolUseId`), potentially missing snake_case fields (`tool_call_id`, `tool_use_id`) from OpenAI-responses tool results. This is now fixed with deduplicated resolveId calls to avoid occurrence-aware ID mismatches.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `rewriteAssistantToolCallIds` skips `type: "tool_use"` (snake_case) blocks because it only checks camelCase type names. Composite IDs with pipe characters pass through unsanitized, causing Anthropic 400 on cross-provider failover replay.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node v24.13.1, branch `fix/tool-use-id-sanitizer-snakecase-95623`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Proof script: demonstrates before/after behavior + source verification
node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-95623-tool-use-id-sanitizer-proof.mts

# Unit tests (all 26 pass)
pnpm test src/agents/tool-call-id.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix**:

`sanitizeToolCallId` composite ID sanitization:

| Input | Output | Expected | Match |
|-------|--------|----------|-------|
| `call_abc123|fc_456` | `callabc123fc456` | `callabc123fc456` | ✓ |

Live sanitization with snake_case `type: "tool_use"` blocks:

| Check | Result |
|-------|--------|
| Input changed | YES ✓ |
| Block 1 type recognized (`tool_use`) | YES ✓ |
| Block 2 type recognized (`tool_use`) | YES ✓ |
| Block 1 id sanitized | YES ✓ |
| Block 2 id sanitized | YES ✓ |
| Tool result IDs match blocks | YES ✓ |
| CamelCase `toolUse` still recognized | YES ✓ |

Snake_case tool result field rewrite check:

| Check | Result |
|-------|--------|
| `toolCallId` rewritten | YES ✓ |
| `tool_call_id` rewritten | YES ✓ |
| `toolUseId` rewritten | YES ✓ |
| `tool_use_id` rewritten | YES ✓ |

Source code verification:

| Check | Result |
|-------|--------|
| `TOOL_CALL_TYPES` includes `tool_use` | YES ✓ |
| `TOOL_CALL_TYPES` includes `toolUse` | YES ✓ |
| Type guard uses `TOOL_CALL_TYPES.has()` | YES ✓ |
| Old hardcoded guard removed | YES ✓ |
| `rewriteToolResultIds` reads `tool_call_id` | YES ✓ |
| `rewriteToolResultIds` writes `tool_use_id` | YES ✓ |

Unit tests:

```bash
$ pnpm test src/agents/tool-call-id.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  26 passed (26)
```

**Observed result after fix**:

1. `TOOL_CALL_TYPES` includes all 6 variants (camelCase + snake_case)
2. Type guard uses `TOOL_CALL_TYPES.has(type)` — format-agnostic
3. `rewriteToolResultIds` handles `tool_call_id` and `tool_use_id` alongside existing fields
4. Composite IDs properly sanitized (pipes removed)
5. CamelCase types continue working (regression check passes)
6. All 26 unit tests pass


## Root cause (if applicable)

The type guard in `rewriteAssistantToolCallIds` (`tool-call-id.ts:415`) used hardcoded camelCase strings: `(type !== "functionCall" && type !== "toolUse" && type !== "toolCall")`. When OpenAI-responses cross-provider failover produces assistant content blocks with `type: "tool_use"` (snake_case), the block is not recognized as a tool call and is returned unmodified. The composite ID `call_XXX|fc_YYY` with pipe character survives to the Anthropic target, which rejects with 400.

The call chain: OpenAI-responses records persist with snake_case blocks → cross-provider failover to Anthropic targets → step 8 (`normalizeOpenAIResponsesToolCallIds`, which converts to camelCase) is skipped because `isOpenAIResponsesApi` is false → step 9 (`sanitizeToolCallIdsForCloudCodeAssist`) runs on raw snake_case blocks → old hardcoded guard skips them → composite IDs unsanitized.

The fix adds all 6 variants to `TOOL_CALL_TYPES` and replaces the hardcoded check with `TOOL_CALL_TYPES.has(type)`. `rewriteToolResultIds` is also extended to read/write `tool_call_id` and `tool_use_id`.

## Regression Test Plan

- `tool-call-id.test.ts` (26 tests): Covers all sanitization scenarios including snake_case blocks, snake_case tool result fields, composite IDs, collision handling, native ID preservation, and replay-safe thinking
- Minimal risk: only `TOOL_CALL_TYPES` set and two downstream consumers modified

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Cross-provider failover sessions (e.g., OpenAI → Anthropic) that previously bricked with 400 errors on tool call ID format can now complete successfully.

## Security Impact

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes — only adding new type variants, all originals unchanged
- [x] Config/env changes? No
- [x] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes — targets the root cause at both the type guard and tool result field level
- **Alternative considered**: (a) Adding only snake_case strings to hardcoded check — rejected because `TOOL_CALL_TYPES` already exists for other consumers, so using it in the type guard is the consistent approach

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Fixes #95623

target.title: Fix Gemini web search freshness handling
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Gemini-backed `web_search` could fail before returning any grounded result when callers used `freshness: "day"` / `pd`.

The failing path was OpenClaw mapping the 24-hour freshness shortcut to a hard Gemini `google_search.timeRangeFilter`. Live Gemini API checks showed that exact 24-hour windows are currently rejected with `FIELD_INVALID`, while wider hard ranges still work.

This PR also keeps the two lint fixes from the closed #95638 branch, so the current branch remains CI-ready:

- remove the unused `CONTEXT_LIMIT_TRUNCATION_NOTICE` import
- mark the fire-and-forget Codex app-server event emission with `void`

## Why This Change Was Made

The Gemini freshness handling is intentionally narrow:

- `freshness: "day"` / `pd` now adds a soft "last 24 hours" recency instruction to the Gemini query instead of sending a hard 24-hour `timeRangeFilter`
- `freshness: "week"`, `"month"`, and `"year"` still use hard Gemini Google Search grounding `timeRangeFilter` values
- explicit `date_after` / `date_before` ranges still use hard `timeRangeFilter` values
- Gemini cache keys partition no freshness, soft day freshness, and hard freshness/date ranges independently

This avoids the invalid 24-hour Gemini request without weakening wider freshness or explicit date-range contracts.

## User Impact

Agents can use `web_search` with Gemini and `freshness: "day"` again without hitting the Gemini endpoint validation error.

Users who rely on stricter wider ranges still get hard Gemini grounding filters for `week`, `month`, `year`, and explicit date ranges.

The lint cleanup has no intended runtime behavior change.

## Evidence

Automated checks:

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/google/web-search-provider.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-result-context-guard.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/google/web-search-provider.test.ts extensions/google/src/gemini-web-search-provider.runtime.ts extensions/google/src/gemini-web-search-provider.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/google/src/gemini-web-search-provider.ts extensions/google/src/gemini-web-search-provider.runtime.ts extensions/google/web-search-provider.test.ts docs/tools/gemini-search.md docs/tools/web.md extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-result-context-guard.ts`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/tools/gemini-search.md docs/tools/web.md`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check`
- `codex review --base origin/main`

Live EC2/OpenClaw agent check:

- session: `c69160d0-9bc2-4dba-8cc9-1d2bcb30005a`
- tool call: `web_search` with `freshness: "day"`
- provider/model: `gemini` / `gemini-2.5-flash`
- result: tool summary failures `0`
- caveat: the EC2 installed `dist` already contained the freshness-hint patch, so this proves patched-agent behavior, not an unpatched upstream baseline

Direct Gemini endpoint checks from the same EC2 runtime, with credentials redacted:

- `2026-06-21T03:25:10Z` -> `2026-06-22T03:25:10Z`: HTTP 400, `GenerateContentRequest.tools[0].google_search.time_range_filter: [FIELD_INVALID] Invalid time range: end_time must be 24 hours after start_time.`
- `2026-06-20T00:00:00Z` -> `2026-06-21T00:00:00Z`: HTTP 400 with the same validation error
- `2026-06-21T00:00:00Z` -> `2026-06-23T00:00:00Z`: HTTP 200, grounded response returned
- `2026-06-15T00:00:00Z` -> `2026-06-22T00:00:00Z`: HTTP 200, grounded response returned

Reviewer feedback addressed:

- the branch no longer softens all freshness shortcuts
- hard Gemini freshness is preserved where the live endpoint accepts it
- lint cleanup remains here because #95638 is closed

## AI Assistance

This PR was prepared with AI assistance and validated with targeted automated checks plus redacted live EC2/Gemini endpoint evidence.

target.title: test(qa-lab): harden whatsapp qa scenarios
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The WhatsApp QA lane was missing coverage for several visible plugin behaviors, and a few existing scenarios were not proving the exact behavior users see in WhatsApp.

The clearest example was quoted replies: the old quote-reply scenario could pass without verifying that the outgoing WhatsApp message actually quoted the correct inbound message. That left room for the reply quote feature itself to regress while QA still passed.

This PR hardens the WhatsApp QA lane by adding more real WhatsApp scenarios, tightening existing ones, and adding the small runtime/test support needed for those scenarios to prove the right behavior.

## Why This Change Was Made

### New WhatsApp QA scenarios

This adds coverage for user-visible WhatsApp behaviors that could previously regress without a focused scenario catching them:

- `whatsapp-agent-message-action-react`: sends a normal WhatsApp DM, lets the agent use the `message` tool, and verifies the native WhatsApp emoji reaction appears on the trigger message.
- `whatsapp-agent-message-action-upload-file`: drives an agent `message(action=upload-file)` path and verifies visible WhatsApp media is sent.
- `whatsapp-reply-to-mode-batched`: sends two messages close together and verifies the agent can see the earlier queued message in the same run context.
- `whatsapp-status-reaction-lifecycle`: verifies the visible status-reaction sequence around ack/reply cleanup, including order.
- `whatsapp-group-pending-history-context`: protects group pending-history context so queued group messages remain available to the agent.
- `whatsapp-broadcast-group-fanout`: verifies one mentioned group message can produce distinct visible replies from two configured agents.
- `whatsapp-group-activation-always`: changes a group to `/activation always`, verifies an unmentioned group message wakes the agent, then restores `/activation mention`.
- `whatsapp-group-reply-to-bot-triggers`: seeds a bot reply and verifies a native quoted reply to that bot message can wake the agent without an explicit mention.
- `whatsapp-inbound-reaction-no-trigger`: verifies an inbound user reaction is observed as a reaction event and does not start a fresh agent run.

### Existing scenario improvements

This also tightens scenarios that already existed but were not strict enough:

- Reply/quote delivery now verifies the actual quoted WhatsApp message id, including long final replies that are split into multiple chunks.
- Batched reply-to-mode can no longer pass as a normal second-message reply; the mock provider only returns the success marker when both messages are present in the same request context.
- Status reaction matching now enforces observed event order instead of accepting the right events in the wrong sequence.
- Group activation restore now runs even if the visible probe fails, so the QA lane does not leave the test group in `/activation always`.
- Broadcast QA now stages mock auth for all broadcast agents, not only the default QA agents.
- WhatsApp evidence now records each scenario posture (`user-path`, `direct-gateway`, or `native-approval`) so scenario results are easier to interpret.

### WhatsApp plugin support fixes

The scenario work exposed a couple of plugin-side gaps that needed to be fixed for the QA lane to test real behavior:

- The WhatsApp QA driver now keeps group identity and participant identity separate. Group messages keep `fromJid` as the group JID while exposing `participantJid` and participant phone metadata separately.
- WhatsApp same-chat `message` actions can infer the current chat from tool context for reactions and file uploads while still using the existing target authorization path.

## User Impact

Users should not see a new command or configuration surface from this PR. The impact is better regression protection for WhatsApp behavior that users do see: quote replies, reactions, media sends, group activation, group reply context, broadcast fanout, status reactions, and inbound reaction handling.

For maintainers, WhatsApp QA should now give a much clearer signal when changes affect visible WhatsApp behavior instead of only proving low-level Gateway calls.

## Evidence

Local verification:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/evidence-summary.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/whatsapp-live.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/providers/mock-openai/server.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/qa-driver.runtime.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-react-action.test.ts`

target.title: feat(config): coerce whole-value ${VAR} JSON arrays/objects in env substitution
target.body:
<!-- AI-assisted: drafted with AI assistance; evidence below is from a human-run live invocation of the config loader and write-back path. -->

Closes #

## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a problem where array or object config cannot be supplied through an environment variable. Config env substitution (`resolveConfigEnvVars`) only ever returns strings, so a value written as `"${VAR}"` resolves to the env var's raw text. For config keys that expect an array or object (allowlists, id lists, structured limits), operators who want to keep that value out of the committed config file and inject it from the environment end up with a string where the schema expects an array/object, which fails validation.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds an **explicit opt-in** modifier: `${VAR:json}`. Only this form coerces — when a value is exactly one `${VAR:json}` reference and the resolved text is a JSON array or object literal, it is parsed into a real array/object. Plain `${VAR}` always stays a string (so existing string fields are never affected), and embedded references (`"prefix-${VAR:json}"`), multi-var strings, scalars, JSON primitives, and malformed JSON keep their string result. Unknown modifiers (`${VAR:foo}`) are left as literal placeholders.

The earlier revision of this PR coerced any whole-value `${VAR}` globally; review correctly flagged two compatibility problems, both now addressed:
- **Schema-blind coercion (fixed):** coercion is now opt-in via `${VAR:json}`, so string-typed fields such as `env.vars` (`z.record(z.string(), z.string())`) keep their string value even when the env value looks like JSON.
- **Config write-back preservation (fixed):** `restoreEnvVarRefs` (`env-preserve.ts`) now restores a sole `${VAR:json}` template when the incoming value is the env-resolved array/object, so the authored env reference survives config round-trips instead of being inlined into `openclaw.json`. The substitution and preservation paths share the same token helpers to avoid grammar drift.

## User Impact

Operators can inject array/object config through environment variables by opting in with `${VAR:json}`. For example, with `ALLOW_FROM='["alice","bob"]'`, a config value of `"${ALLOW_FROM:json}"` resolves to the array `["alice","bob"]`. Plain `${VAR}` behavior, escaping (`$${VAR}`), and missing-var handling are unchanged, and authored `${VAR:json}` references are preserved across config writes. No migration is required.

## Evidence

Live terminal output from running the config loader (`resolveConfigEnvVars`) and the write-back path (`restoreEnvVarRefs`) from source via `node`, against a config file with `allowFrom: "${ALLOW_FROM:json}"` (opt-in) and a plain string-only `env.vars.TAGS: "${TAGS}"`, with `ALLOW_FROM='["alice","bob"]'` and `TAGS='["x","y"]'`. Console output:

```
[1] resolved config:
{
  "channels": { "discord": { "allowFrom": [ "alice", "bob" ] } },
  "env": { "vars": { "TAGS": "[\"x\",\"y\"]" } }
}
opt-in allowFrom isArray: true
plain TAGS stays string: true
[2] write-back (what gets persisted to disk):
{
  "channels": { "discord": { "allowFrom": "${ALLOW_FROM:json}" } },
  "env": { "vars": { "TAGS": "${TAGS}" } }
}
allowFrom ref preserved: true
```

This demonstrates all three guarantees: (1) `${ALLOW_FROM:json}` coerces to a real array; (2) plain `${TAGS}` stays a string even though its value is JSON-looking (string fields are safe); (3) on write-back the authored `${ALLOW_FROM:json}` reference is restored rather than inlined.

Focused tests: `src/config/env-substitution.test.ts` and `src/config/env-preserve.test.ts` (92 passed total), covering opt-in coercion, plain-`${VAR}` passthrough, embedded/scalar/malformed/primitive passthrough, unknown-modifier literal passthrough, and `${VAR:json}` write-back round-trip (restore + caller-changed-value cases). `pnpm tsgo` is clean.

target.title: fix(memory-core): skip forced sync for healthy zero-hit search
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A valid memory search can return zero hits while the index is marked dirty even though the index is otherwise healthy. Before this fix, the tool wrapper treated that dirty-only zero-hit as a reason to call `manager.sync({ reason: "search", force: true })` and wait inside the tool call. On hosts where the forced sync does not complete quickly, `memory_search` can hang until the tool timeout even though the correct user-facing answer is simply an empty result set.

This patch keeps the recall-preserving retry for non-QMD dirty indexes while preventing the QMD healthy dirty-only zero-hit path from forcing a blocking sync. Missing, invalid, empty, or unavailable index states still force sync.

## Summary

- skip blocking forced sync for healthy QMD zero-hit search results, including dirty-only QMD status
- preserve dirty zero-hit forced-sync retry for non-QMD or unknown backends
- keep forced sync for missing, invalid, empty, vector-unavailable, or FTS-unavailable memory states
- treat disabled vector/FTS modes as healthy instead of unavailable
- add regression coverage for QMD dirty healthy zero-hit no-sync and non-QMD dirty zero-hit retry behavior

## Evidence

Focused local verification on branch head `925495a4`:

```text
git diff --check
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /home/lestat/openclaw-fresh/workspace/external/openclaw-source-preservation

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  19 passed (19)
   Start at  20:29:09
   Duration  4.21s (transform 2.03s, setup 283ms, import 3.64s, tests 101ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 19.33s
```

Regression coverage now includes:

- `shouldForceSyncAfterZeroResults` returns `healthy_zero_hit` for QMD dirty healthy zero-hit status
- `shouldForceSyncAfterZeroResults` returns `dirty_index` for builtin or unknown dirty healthy zero-hit status
- `returns a healthy zero-hit without forcing sync when the index is ready` asserts one search call and zero sync calls
- `preserves the dirty zero-hit retry for non-qmd backends` asserts the wrapper syncs once, searches twice, and returns the second-search result
- disabled vector/FTS modes with `available: false` do not make the index unhealthy

Live installed-runtime proof before source promotion:

- `openclaw memory status --json` reported indexed and ready memory with `files: 159`, `chunks: 2519`, `dirty: false`, enabled/available FTS and vector, and `custom.indexIdentity.status: valid`
- a synthetic valid no-hit returned `0` results in about `491.6ms` with `forcedSync.attempted=false` and `forcedSync.reason=healthy_zero_hit` after gateway reload

## Safety Boundaries

- no model switch
- no production reindex
- no vector deletion
- no memory config change
- no secret/token output
- no unrelated Pi-hole/plugin work

target.title: feat(mattermost): persist participated threads for mention-free follow-ups
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

In Mattermost channels/groups the bot drops any message that doesn't explicitly @-mention it (`requireMention` defaults to `true`), and it keeps **no memory of threads it is already conversing in**. So a user must re-mention the bot on *every* follow-up in an active thread. Sibling channels already solve this — Slack tracks participated threads (in-memory + SQLite) and feeds a `bot_thread_participant` implicit-mention into the shared gate. Mattermost was the outlier: it even passes the thread root id into its mention gate but never reads it.

## Why This Change Was Made

Bring Mattermost to parity with Slack's thread-participation behaviour, persisted so it survives a gateway restart. Scoped deliberately to a **bounded, Mattermost-local** change (no Slack edits; keep the existing `evaluateMattermostMentionGate`) per maintainer direction.

## User Impact

- After the bot posts a visible reply in a thread, later messages in that thread are answered **without** a re-mention — matching Slack.
- **Survives restart:** participation is mirrored to the SQLite plugin-state store (namespace `mattermost.thread-participation`, **7-day TTL**, bounded entries), so the bot no longer "forgets" active threads on restart. TTL refreshes on every reply, so active threads stay engaged and only genuinely idle ones expire.
- Always-on (matches Slack's default); **no new config**. DMs unchanged (already mention-free). Threads the bot only *observed* (reasoning-only / empty / suppressed outcomes) do **not** auto-engage.
- Best-effort persistence: if the store is unavailable it degrades to in-memory only and never blocks message handling.

## Evidence

- New `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/thread-participation.ts` mirrors Slack's `sent-thread-cache.ts` (dual layer: `resolveGlobalDedupeCache` + `runtime.state.openKeyedStore`).
- Gate: `evaluateMattermostMentionGate` gains a `threadAlreadyEngaged` input, folded into `effectiveWasMentioned` and the onchar-not-triggered guard.
- Monitor: awaits the participation check before the gate; records after a visible thread reply (`outcome` `text`/`media`) keyed by `effectiveReplyToId`.
- Tests: the two touched test files pass — **2 files, 12 tests** — covering remember/lookup, account/channel/thread isolation, **persist→reload across a simulated restart**, graceful degradation when the store fails, and the new gate cases (engaged thread bypasses mention + onchar; non-engaged still drops with `missing-mention`).
- Build: `pnpm build` clean (`tsdown` OK, no `[INEFFECTIVE_DYNAMIC_IMPORT]` for the lazy monitor boundary).

### Follow-ups (tracked separately, not in this PR)
- **reply-to-bot parity** — engage when a user replies directly to the bot's own message (Slack/Telegram already do this); queued as its own session.
- **Shared extraction** — Slack's and this cache are near-identical; a future change can extract one channel-generic helper and migrate both. Slack still ships a 24h TTL, so that extraction is the natural place to standardise the window across channels.

target.title: fix(doctor): skip groupAllowFrom migration for schemaless external channels
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Upgrading OpenClaw 2026.6.8 → 2026.6.9 corrupts the external ClawMail `email`
channel config. The doctor migration `maybeRepairGroupAllowFromFallback` injects
a `groupAllowFrom` key into `channels.email.accounts.default`, which the email
plugin's schema rejects, so the Gateway fails to start:

```
Config validation failed: channels.email.accounts.default: invalid config for plugin email: must not have additional properties: "groupAllowFrom"
```

Fixes #95515. (Supersedes #95518, which was auto-closed for the author's active-PR
queue limit, not on merit.)

## Why This Change Was Made

`generatedSchemaAllowsGroupAllowFrom` defaulted to **permissive** (`!schema || …`)
when a channel had no entry in the bundled channel-config metadata. `email` is an
external plugin (ClawMail) — it is not in this repo and is absent from
`GENERATED_BUNDLED_CHANNEL_CONFIG_METADATA` — so core cannot verify its schema and
must not write keys into it. Per the owner boundary in `AGENTS.md`
("plugin-owned config repairs live in that plugin's doctor contract"), external
plugin config repair belongs to that plugin's own doctor, not core.

The schema guard is the correct, minimal fix: it now **fails closed** when core
cannot confirm the target schema accepts the key. The capability default stays
permissive for warnings; only actual config *writes* are now schema-verified. This
is the only place core can fix this, since the email plugin lives outside the repo.

## User Impact

- Bundled channels (e.g. `feishu`) still migrate exactly as before — no regression.
- External channels with no bundled schema are left untouched on upgrade, so the
  Gateway no longer crash-loops after 2026.6.8 → 2026.6.9.

## Evidence

real-behavior proof — drove the actual `maybeRepairGroupAllowFromFallback` with the
exact config from the issue (external `email` account alongside a bundled `feishu`
channel), comparing current `main` behavior against the fix:

BEFORE (fix reverted, current `main` behavior) — reproduces the bug:
```
[repro95515] email.account after: {"allowFrom":["*"],"groupAllowFrom":["*"]}
[repro95515] changes: [
  "channels.feishu.groupAllowFrom: copied 1 sender entry ...",
  "channels.email.accounts.default.groupAllowFrom: copied 1 sender entry ..."
]
```

AFTER (this fix) — email untouched, feishu still migrated (no regression):
```
[repro95515] email.account after: {"allowFrom":["*"]}
[repro95515] changes: [
  "channels.feishu.groupAllowFrom: copied 1 sender entry ..."
]
```

Unit tests: `allowfrom-fallback-migration.test.ts` 7/7 pass (adds a case asserting
external schemaless channels are skipped). Format (`oxfmt --check`) and lint
(`oxlint`) clean on the changed files.

target.title: fix(agents): expand embedded fallback classifier to accept transient provider errors
target.body:
## Summary

When the primary provider returns an `upstream_error` (e.g. "Upstream request failed"), OpenClaw ends the turn with "LLM request failed" instead of trying configured fallback models. The root cause is a two-layer gap: the error classifier only recognized `auth`/`billing` payloads as fallback-worthy, and the message pattern matcher did not include "upstream request failed" as a recognized server error.

This PR fixes both layers:

1. **Classifier filter** (`result-fallback-classifier.ts`): Renames `classifyBusinessDenialErrorPayloadReason` → `classifyFailoverErrorPayloadReason` and expands the accepted reasons set to include `rate_limit`, `overloaded`, `server_error`, and `timeout` alongside the existing `auth`/`auth_permanent`/`billing`. Uses a `Set<FailoverReason>` for clarity and maintainability.

2. **Pattern matcher** (`failover-matches.ts`): Adds "upstream request failed" to the `serverError` patterns list so that the bare text error from providers is recognized by `classifyFailoverClassificationFromMessage`.

3. **Structured JSON matcher** (`errors.ts`): Adds `"type":"upstream_error"` to `isStructuredServerErrorMessage` so that full JSON error payloads with this type are classified as `server_error`.

Fixes #95519

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Provider-side transient errors (upstream_error, HTTP 500, rate_limit, overload) now trigger the configured fallback chain instead of ending the turn with "LLM request failed".

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node v24.16.0, Linux x86_64
- **Code analysis path**: See [issue-95519-01-analysis.md](docs/.local/issue-95519-01-analysis.md)
- **Test framework**: Vitest with project test config

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Run fallback classifier tests
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.test.ts

# Run regression tests for related modules
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts src/agents/outcome-fallback-runtime-contract.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

| Test | Before | After |
|------|--------|-------|
| Transient server-error payloads | `null` (no fallback) | `timeout` fallback triggered |
| "Upstream request failed" text | `null` (no fallback) | `timeout` fallback triggered |
| `{"type":"upstream_error",...}` JSON | `null` (no fallback) | `server_error` fallback triggered |
| Business-denial payloads (auth/billing) | fallback triggered | fallback triggered (unchanged) |
| All 13 classifier tests | pass | pass |
| 92 model-fallback + outcome-fallback tests | pass | pass |

**Git stats**:
```bash
5 files changed, 80 insertions(+), 14 deletions(-)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: 
The fallback classifier now correctly identifies transient provider errors as fallback-eligible. Previously, `classifyFailoverErrorPayloadReason("Upstream request failed")` returned `null`; now it returns `"timeout"`, which triggers the configured fallback chain.

**What was not tested**: 
- Live gateway/provider reproduction was not performed (requires real provider credentials with specific error conditions)
- Other non-transient error categories (format, model_not_found, session_expired) remain unchanged — they correctly do not trigger fallback
- Cross-provider edge cases: the fix uses the shared `classifyFailoverReason` infrastructure, so all provider text variants benefit from the expanded classification

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests (classifier) | ✅ 13/13 passing | 3 new test cases added: transient server error, upstream request text, upstream_error JSON |
| Unit Tests (model-fallback) | ✅ pass | 92 tests passing, no regressions |
| Unit Tests (outcome-fallback) | ✅ pass | Included in above |
| Regression (helpers) | ✅ 180/183 passing | 3 pre-existing failures in sandbox tool-policy audit (unrelated) |
| Type Check | ⏳ Pending CI | TypeScript compilation verified locally |

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** `Yes` — Transient provider errors (upstream_error, rate_limit, overloaded, server_error, timeout) now trigger the configured fallback model chain instead of ending the turn. This matches documented fallback behavior.

**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** `No` — No configuration schema changes, no environment variables, no database migrations.

**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** `No` — Only affects fallback classification logic. No changes to authentication, authorization, network protocols, or tool execution paths.

**What is the highest-risk area?**
- Broadening the accepted failover reasons could cause fallback to trigger for errors that should NOT be fallback-eligible (e.g., schema/format errors masquerading as transient errors)

**How is that risk mitigated?**
- The fix uses an explicit allowlist (`FALLBACKABLE_PROVIDER_ERROR_REASONS`) — only `auth`, `auth_permanent`, `billing`, `rate_limit`, `overloaded`, `server_error`, and `timeout` are allowed
- Non-transient reasons (`format`, `model_not_found`, `empty_response`, `unclassified`) are explicitly excluded
- Existing guards (visible output check, hook block check, side-effect check, abort check) remain in place
- The "upstream request failed" pattern addition is a narrow substring match that only applies to server Error pattern matching

## Current review state

**What is the next action?**
- Maintainer review requested
- CI validation pending (tests will run automatically on PR creation)

**Related contributors** (based on ClawSweeper analysis):
- steipete: Recent result-fallback classifier and model-failover docs work defining this fallback boundary
- vincentkoc: Recent area contributor for result-fallback-classifier.ts and model-fallback.ts

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
