target.title: feat(llm): add Inworld as built-in llm provider
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Inworld is currently a TTS-only plugin in OpenClaw. Users cannot use Inworld as an LLM provider for chat completions, despite Inworld offering an OpenAI-compatible LLM API with routing, tool calls, and reasoning.

Why does this matter now?

TTS integration landed in #55972. LLM completes the provider, making Inworld a single-key solution for both speech and inference.

What is the intended outcome?

`openclaw onboard --auth-choice inworld-api-key` configures Inworld as an LLM provider; `inworld/auto` resolves via live catalog discovery and completes against `https://api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions`. Streaming, tool calls, and per-model reasoning levels all work out of the box.

What is intentionally out of scope?

Custom base URL override for the LLM surface, multi-key rotation, embeddings/image/video/STT endpoints, UI onboarding graphics, surfacing Inworld's response `metadata` (routing diagnostics) back into openclaw cost attribution.

What does success look like?

Live catalog (`/llm/v1alpha/models`) populates on authenticated startup, completions (streaming + non-streaming + tool calls) work, per-model reasoning levels map correctly to openclaw effort ids, existing TTS behavior is unchanged.

What should reviewers focus on?

Plugin manifest shape additions, catalog discovery caching strategy (process-lifetime, no TTL polling), `normalizeResolvedModel` wire-id rewrite for first-party `inworld/models/<NAME>` refs, and `EFFORT_*` → openclaw effort id mapping with per-model `supportedLevels`.

<details>
<summary>Summary guidance</summary>

This PR description is the contributor's durable explanation of the change. Write it for human maintainers first; ClawSweeper and Barnacle use the same text to understand intent, proof, risk, and current review state.

</details>

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Does not close any open issue.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related: #55972 (TTS integration this PR extends). Implementation pattern mirrors the OpenRouter and Chutes provider plugins (live `/llm/v1alpha/models` discovery + small static fallback).

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Inworld team follow-up to the merged TTS PR #55972 to complete the provider integration.

<details>
<summary>Linked context guidance</summary>

Link the issue, PR, discussion, maintainer request, or owner request that explains why this PR should exist.

</details>

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Inworld is now selectable as an LLM provider; chat/stream/tool calls succeed against `api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 25.5.0, Node 24.14, local OpenClaw checkout at the PR head SHA, real Inworld API key against `api.inworld.ai`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/inworld
  curl -sS -X POST -H "Authorization: Basic $INWORLD_API_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
       -d '{"model":"<wire-id>","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Reply OK only."}],"max_tokens":16}' \
       https://api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions
  ```
- **Evidence after fix (live console capture from this branch checkout):**

  ```
  === pnpm test extensions/inworld ===
   RUN  v4.1.7 /Users/.../public/openclaw
   Test Files  6 passed (6)
        Tests  49 passed (49)
     Duration  3.47s

  === Live POST /v1/chat/completions (5 representative models) ===
    OK   inworld/models/GLM-5.1            -> wire-model=inworld/GLM-5.1                          finish=stop credential=system
    OK   anthropic/claude-opus-4-8         -> wire-model=anthropic/claude-opus-4-8                finish=stop credential=system
    OK   openai/gpt-4o                     -> wire-model=openai/gpt-4o                            finish=stop credential=system
    OK   google-ai-studio/gemini-2.5-flash -> wire-model=google-ai-studio/gemini-2.5-flash        finish=stop credential=system
    OK   auto                              -> wire-model=deepinfra/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Flash  finish=stop credential=system
  ```

  Each `wire-model=...` row is the exact `model` field Inworld echoed back, confirming the plugin emits canonical wire ids — including the rewrite from user-facing `inworld/models/GLM-5.1` to wire form `inworld/GLM-5.1`, and Inworld's routing selection for `auto`.

  Separate live catalog probe: `GET /llm/v1alpha/models` returned 124 entries; iterating each through `parseInworldModel` produced 124 valid `ModelDefinitionConfig` results and 0 parse errors.

- **Observed result after fix:** Every probed user ref reaches `api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions` and returns a valid OpenAI-shaped response with `finish_reason: "stop"`. First-party id rewrite is observable in the `wire-model` echo.
- **What was not tested:** Windows/Linux runtime (only macOS), interactive `openclaw onboard` wizard end-to-end through the new auth choice, prompt-cache billing reconciliation against Inworld's invoice surface, full coverage of every reasoning-effort level on every model, every one of the 124 catalog entries individually via chat completions (probed 13 representative models, one per upstream provider).
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Live tests require a valid `INWORLD_API_KEY` with LLM-router access. CI will only run the offline vitest layer without the env var; the gated live test auto-skips when `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST` is unset or the key is missing.
- **Before evidence:** Before this PR, `openclaw onboard --auth-choice inworld-api-key` did not exist; `inworld/<provider>/<model>` refs were unresolvable; `openclaw models list` did not list any Inworld LLM entries. TTS via `messages.tts.providers.inworld` continued to work and is unchanged by this PR.

<details>
<summary>Real behavior proof guidance</summary>

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only.

</details>

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/inworld
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.bundled.config.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
node --import tsx scripts/check-import-cycles.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/inworld
INWORLD_API_KEY=<your-key> node scripts/test-live.mjs -- extensions/inworld/inworld.live.test.ts
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `extensions/inworld/models.test.ts` — catalog parser: pricing scale, modality detection, vision flag, `isSupported: false` rejection, missing-field handling, first-party id storage (no `inworld/` provider prefix), wire-id rewrite, `supportedLevels` capture, prompt-caching flag.
- `extensions/inworld/thinking-policy.test.ts` — `EFFORT_*` and lowercase short-form acceptance (forward-compat), per-model `supportedLevels` profile derivation, `medium`-preferred-else-highest default-level logic.
- `extensions/inworld/onboard.test.ts` — allowlist + primary-model side-effects of `applyInworldConfig`.
- `extensions/inworld/index.test.ts` — LLM provider registration alongside speech, fallback-catalog exposure, dynamic resolve for known/unknown ids.
- `extensions/inworld/inworld.live.test.ts` — gated live cases for non-streaming chat, SSE streaming, and tool calling.

What failed before this fix, if known?

N/A — new feature.

If no test was added, why not?

Tests added; see above.

<details>
<summary>Testing guidance</summary>

List focused commands, not every incidental check.

</details>

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes` — `inworld` now appears in `openclaw models list` as an LLM provider; `openclaw onboard --auth-choice inworld-api-key` becomes a valid auth choice; `agents.defaults.model.primary` accepts `inworld/<ref>`. Existing TTS behavior is unchanged.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes` — `extensions/inworld/openclaw.plugin.json` adds `providers`, `providerAuthChoices`, `modelIdNormalization`, `providerEndpoints`, `providerRequest`. `INWORLD_API_KEY` env var was already declared by the TTS PR; no new env vars introduced. `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` regenerated. `extensions/inworld/package.json` is intentionally untouched (npm package name stays `@openclaw/inworld-speech` even though the plugin now serves LLM + TTS).

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No` — same `INWORLD_API_KEY` and HTTP Basic scheme already shipped for TTS, same `fetchWithSsrFGuard` policy scoped to `api.inworld.ai`. No new outbound hosts; no new secret stores. The LLM secret target is auto-covered by the existing generic `models.providers.*.apiKey` wildcard registry entry.

What is the highest-risk area? `normalizeResolvedModel` rewrites first-party model ids (`models/<NAME>` → `inworld/models/<NAME>`) at the wire layer. A bug here would silently send the wrong `model` field to Inworld and break only first-party model requests.

How is that risk mitigated? Pure unit test (`toInworldWireModelId`) asserts the transform in both directions (first-party rewritten, passthrough refs untouched). Live test exercises the catalog-driven path for `inworld/auto` against `api.inworld.ai`. Additional live curl probe verified the first-party id rewrite end-to-end (see `inworld/models/GLM-5.1 -> wire-model=inworld/GLM-5.1` row in the proof block above). Hook only fires for ids starting with `models/`, so non-first-party refs are zero-impact.

<details>
<summary>Risk guidance</summary>

Use this for author judgment that is not obvious from the diff.

</details>

## Current review state

What is the next action? Initial review by maintainers and automated reviewers (ClawSweeper, Codex, greptile).

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Nothing on author side at submission. Open to feedback on the manifest shape and live-discovery caching strategy if reviewers prefer a different pattern than OpenRouter/Chutes.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? Reverted `extensions/inworld/package.json` (kept original `@openclaw/inworld-speech` name) to avoid the dependency-guard CI gate; added pasted live console output to the Real behavior proof section so it isn't pure narration.

<details>
<summary>Review state guidance</summary>

Keep this as the durable state for review progress.

</details>

target.title: fix(opencode): restore Zen model catalog
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the bundled `opencode` Zen provider exposed auth/setup but did not own a Zen model catalog/resolver, so users had to hand-register `opencode/<model>` rows; review also found route/cost/docs drift around MiniMax, static Zen metadata, and the documented Gemini example.
- Solution: restore the Zen provider-owned catalog, dynamic resolver, runtime discovery filter, route normalization, provider-discovery static catalog, manifest anchor metadata, cost metadata, and regression tests at the `opencode` plugin boundary.
- Latest follow-up: this update moves the full 48-row offline Zen catalog behind `providerCatalogEntry`/provider discovery, trims `openclaw.plugin.json` to four representative route/cost anchors, and keeps the documented Gemini example resolvable.
- What changed: the PR now filters live discovery to provider-owned rows only, keeps the curated 48-row seed visible through provider discovery when live discovery is unavailable, routes MiniMax through the Zen OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` path, preserves required `cost` metadata for every runtime row and manifest anchor, and verifies the docs examples are resolvable.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** users can select advertised `opencode/<model>` Zen models without hand-writing every model row, and docs no longer point them at an unresolved Gemini reference.
- **Out of scope:** this does not change non-OpenCode providers, `opencode-go` runtime behavior, generic LLM adapter APIs, the shared OpenCode credential fallback policy, or the public model/config cost contract.
- Fixes #92479

## Origin / follow-up

- **Follows / completes:** This updates the existing #92495 repair branch after ClawSweeper flagged live-only Zen row synthesis, missing successful MiniMax live proof, cost-contract failures, and a stale documented Gemini example.
- **Gap this fills:** The branch now proves the documented examples resolve, refreshes authenticated Zen `/models` proof with a redacted key, and keeps MiniMax on the verified OpenAI-compatible Zen route.
- **Consistency:** The live-discovery behavior matches the shared `buildLiveModelProviderConfig` shape used by `opencode-go`: use live discovery when authenticated rows are available, filter to provider-owned metadata rows, and retain the static seed when discovery is unavailable or not provider-owned. The offline static surface also now mirrors `opencode-go` by exposing the complete catalog through provider discovery instead of forcing every row into the manifest.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- **Linked PR(s):** #92495 is the existing linked PR for #92479; `check-upstream-fixed` found `origin/main` does not already cover this repair.
- **Related open PR / same-contract scan:** The linked issue and ClawSweeper review identify this PR as the active candidate fix. I did not find a same-contract canonical PR on `origin/main` superseding the Zen catalog owner-boundary repair.
- **Gap in linked PR(s):** The current linked PR itself had author-actionable gaps: live-only discovery rows were exposed with inferred metadata, MiniMax was grouped with the Zen Anthropic Messages route, cost metadata briefly violated the required model contract, and docs referenced `gemini-3-pro`, which the provider could not resolve.
- **Why this patch is better/canonical:** It fixes the gaps at the provider-owned catalog/routing/docs boundary instead of adding downstream special cases, publishing guessed live-only rows, or widening the shared model schema from one provider PR.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The bundled `opencode` Zen provider now exposes usable `opencode/<model>` rows without hand-written user config. Documented Zen examples resolve through the provider runtime, authenticated Zen `/models` discovery returns the expected curated rows, unknown live-only IDs are not promoted without owned metadata, MiniMax uses the OpenAI-compatible Zen v1 route, and every static Zen row remains compatible with the required model cost contract.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux, Node/Vitest/pnpm in the local OpenClaw PR worktree. The OpenCode Zen API key was used only as a transient environment variable for live proof; logs redact secret-like strings and the key is not stored in the repo or PR body.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd "$TASK_WORKTREE"

git diff --check
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/opencode/index.test.ts
pnpm docs:list
pnpm run check:test-types
pnpm run tsgo:prod
pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile
pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:canary

OPENCODE_API_KEY=[REDACTED] node --import tsx <redacted live proof runner>
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Focused provider regression tests now include the docs/catalog contract, provider-discovery offline catalog contract, and manifest-anchor cost contract, and pass all 13 tests:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  13 passed (13)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 56.04s
```

The docs list command completed before the type/package-boundary gates, and the type gates completed successfully:

```text
## pnpm docs:list
$ node scripts/docs-list.js
Listing all markdown files in docs folder:
...
## pnpm run check:test-types
$ pnpm tsgo:test
...
## pnpm run tsgo:prod
$ pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:extensions
```

The extension package-boundary checks passed in both compile and canary modes:

```text
extension package boundary check passed
mode: compile
compiled plugins: 115
canary plugins: 0
```

```text
extension package boundary check passed
mode: canary
compiled plugins: 0
canary plugins: 2
```

The fresh authenticated Zen live proof passed with the redacted key:

```text
step=documented_examples_resolve
opencode/claude-opus-4-6 -> api=anthropic-messages baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen
opencode/gpt-5.5 -> api=openai-responses baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1
opencode/gemini-3.1-pro -> api=google-generative-ai baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1
```

```text
step=live_catalog_builder
modelCount=48
requiredIdsPresent.claude-opus-4-6=true
requiredIdsPresent.gpt-5.5=true
requiredIdsPresent.gemini-3.1-pro=true
requiredIdsPresent.minimax-m2.7=true
minimaxRoute.api=openai-completions
minimaxRoute.baseUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1
```

```text
step=authenticated_models_endpoint
status=200
finalUrl=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/models
liveModelCount=48
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.claude-opus-4-6=true
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.gpt-5.5=true
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.gemini-3.1-pro=true
requiredIdsPresentInLiveModels.minimax-m2.7=true
```

```text
step=minimax_openai_compatible_completion
status=200
modelId=minimax-m2.7

endpoint=https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/chat/completions
assistantTextLength=324
```

```text
step=live_proof_result
result=PASS
checks=documented OpenCode Zen examples resolve through provider runtime; runtime live catalog builder consumes authenticated Zen /models rows through its fetch guard and returns required curated rows; GET https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/models succeeds and includes required curated rows; minimax-m2.7 uses openai-completions at https://opencode.ai/zen/v1 and returns non-empty assistant content
```

The cost-contract summary remains satisfied: the 48-row provider-discovery seed and the four manifest anchors have no missing `cost` fields, tiered public-doc rows keep `tieredPricing`, and free rows are explicit zero-cost rows.

- **Observed result after fix:** `opencode` now exposes the Zen catalog, `opencode/gemini-3.1-pro` is the documented resolvable Gemini example, authenticated Zen `/models` returns the required curated rows, `minimax-m2.7` no longer uses the failed Anthropic Messages path, and the current MiniMax route returns a successful OpenAI-compatible chat completion.
- **What was not tested:** This latest live proof does not re-run paid completions for every Zen family; it focuses on authenticated catalog discovery, documented example resolution, provider-discovery static catalog coverage, and the MiniMax route ClawSweeper specifically challenged. Earlier evidence on this branch covered representative Claude/GPT/Gemini/Qwen/MiniMax live completions. I also did not change or prove final product decisions for the full 48-row seed membership or the shared `opencode`/`opencode-go` credential fallback policy.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 — 48-row static seed drift risk:** Reduced the manifest ownership surface by moving the complete 48-row offline catalog behind `providerCatalogEntry: "./provider-discovery.ts"`, while keeping `openclaw.plugin.json` to four representative route/cost anchors. The focused test now proves provider discovery exposes all 48 rows and the manifest anchors still match runtime cost metadata.
- **RF-002 — shared OpenCode credential fallback policy:** Left the existing shared `opencode`/`opencode-go` credential fallback unchanged because it matches current docs and sibling behavior; the split-key policy remains out of scope for #87790.
- **RF-003 — current-head CI/check completion:** The latest visible current-head CI, CodeQL Critical Quality, OpenGrep, labeler, dependency guard, security guard, workflow sanity, and Real behavior proof runs are passing for `4520f97490ee1492f71ba4f944e36166f6c2664a`; the earlier queued/cancelled entries are stale workflow noise, not current patch failures.
- **RF-004 — maintainer decision boundary:** The remaining provider-owner decision is now narrower: accept the provider-owned 48-row TS source-of-truth and four manifest anchors, instead of accepting a full 48-row user-visible manifest blob. No automated repair is needed for the split-key policy decision.
- **RF-005 — real behavior proof refresh:** Addressed with fresh redacted OpenCode Zen live proof in `opencode-zen-live-proof-20260622.log`: authenticated `/models` returned 48 rows including the documented examples and `minimax-m2.7`, and MiniMax returned a 200 OpenAI-compatible chat completion on `https://opencode.ai/zen/v1/chat/completions`.
- **Previously addressed MiniMax route and live-only row synthesis findings:** Still fixed. MiniMax M2.7 remains on the OpenAI-compatible chat completions route, and live-only rows are filtered through provider-owned metadata instead of guessed into selectable runtime rows.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `extensions/opencode/index.test.ts`, plus type/package-boundary gates and redacted live evidence stored outside the repo worktree.
- **Scenario locked in:** Regression tests assert provider discovery exposes the complete 48-row offline Zen catalog, the manifest keeps only four representative route/cost anchors, dynamic model resolution works for Zen IDs, documented `docs/providers/opencode.md` Zen examples are resolvable, shared `opencode-go` credentials can populate the `opencode` catalog path, Claude/GPT/Gemini/Qwen/MiniMax families route to the expected adapter/base URL pairs, live-only discovery rows are filtered until metadata is owned, every provider-discovery/runtime row and manifest anchor has required cost metadata, and static fallback remains visible when live discovery is unavailable or returns no owned IDs.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A focused provider test locks the source-of-truth catalog/routing/docs/cost contract without broad adapter mocks, while live proof covers the external Zen catalog and the exact MiniMax route that review requested.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** `merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility`, `merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider`, provider catalog/routing, provider-discovery metadata, manifest anchors, docs accuracy, and catalog seed review cost.
- **Risk explanation:** The compatibility risk is that a provider-owned seed can drift from Zen if the upstream catalog changes; this is reduced by live discovery and by filtering to owned metadata. The auth-provider risk is bounded to resolving the same OpenCode credential across `opencode` and `opencode-go`; no key is stored in the repo or PR body. The manifest review risk is reduced because the user-visible manifest now carries only four representative anchors while the complete offline catalog lives in the provider-owned TS discovery source.
- **Why acceptable:** The previous behavior left Zen effectively unusable without hand registration. This patch restores the provider contract at the owner boundary, keeps live discovery as the preferred source when credentials are present, prevents live-only metadata synthesis, preserves a no-discovery local seed through provider discovery, fixes the concrete MiniMax routing defect, and keeps docs examples aligned with resolvable model rows.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The `opencode` Zen provider was migrated as an auth/setup surface without owning the Zen model catalog contract that `opencode-go` already supported. Zen live discovery returns ID rows, so unowned IDs cannot safely become rich selectable rows without provider-owned metadata. MiniMax was also grouped with Claude/Qwen on the Zen Anthropic Messages route even though live Zen behavior proves MiniMax runs through the OpenAI-compatible chat completions route. The docs then referenced a Gemini row that was not present in the provider-owned catalog.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The patch restores the missing provider-owned catalog/resolver boundary, keeps rich metadata owned by the provider seed, fixes MiniMax route selection before downstream adapter execution, keeps the existing required cost contract, and makes docs examples test against the same resolver users rely on.
- **Fix classification:** Root-cause provider catalog, routing, docs, and model-cost contract repair for the bundled `opencode` Zen provider.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for author-actionable source repair: catalog restoration, live-only synthesis filtering, MiniMax route proof, cost-contract coverage, docs/example regression, type gates, package-boundary checks, and fresh authenticated Zen live proof are all covered.
- **Patch quality notes:** The fallback/default warnings are intentional provider-catalog boundary behavior, not symptom masking: live Zen discovery is advisory, provider-owned static rows remain visible through provider discovery when discovery is unavailable, and unknown live-only IDs are filtered rather than guessed into selectable rich model rows. This follow-up reduces manifest review cost by moving the full offline catalog out of `openclaw.plugin.json` and into the provider-owned TS discovery source.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The `opencode` provider plugin is the source-of-truth owner for bundled Zen catalog metadata, route selection, docs examples, provider discovery, and manifest anchor synchronization. This patch fixes the provider boundary before downstream adapter selection instead of adding mirrored runtime records, generic adapter fallbacks, or user-config special cases.
- **Out of scope:** final provider-owner approval of the full 48-row seed membership, split-key policy changes for `opencode` versus `opencode-go`, non-OpenCode provider behavior, and schema-wide model cost contract changes.

target.title: fix: inbound metadata leakage — missing sentinels and display gaps
target.body:
Fixes #93966

## Summary

Inbound metadata blocks (Conversation info, Sender info, etc.) prepended by `buildInboundUserContextPrefix` leaked into user-visible chat in TUI, webchat, and macOS surfaces.

## Root Cause

`stripInboundMetadata` uses `SENTINEL_FAST_RE` to detect and strip metadata blocks. The regex was missing certain sentinel patterns, allowing some blocks to pass through.

## Changes

- `src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.ts`: Fix sentinel matching
- `src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.test.ts`: Add regression tests for lookalike user text

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Injected inbound metadata is now correctly stripped from user-visible chat, while ordinary user text resembling metadata patterns is preserved.

**Evidence after fix**:

```text
$ npx tsx -e '...'
Input:
  Conversation info (untrusted metadata):
  ```json
  {"sender":"+1555000"}
  ```
  Hello, how can I help you?

Output:
  Hello, how can I help you?

Metadata stripped: ✅
User text preserved: ✅
Clean text (no metadata) unchanged: ✅
```

**What was not tested**: End-to-end with a real multi-channel setup.

## Risk

- [x] Backwards compatible (regression tests added)
- [x] Ordinary user text preserved

Closes: #93966

target.title: fix(codex): keep OpenClaw exec when native surface has no environment (#92238)
target.body:
## Summary

Isolated cron jobs on the OpenAI/Codex runtime (gpt-5.5 oauth, official endpoint) report `exec tool unavailable` even though `openclaw sandbox explain` shows `exec` is allowed. The same isolated cron job succeeds on a Google model with identical config. Fixes #92238.

### Root cause

With the 2026.6 "OpenAI Codex folded into `openai`" routing, an `openai` run on the official endpoint resolves to the Codex app-server harness (`openAIProviderUsesCodexRuntimeByDefault` -> `src/agents/harness/policy.ts`), not the embedded Responses transport. Google stays on the embedded OpenClaw runtime where `exec` is a normal tool, which is why the control run works.

The Codex app-server strips OpenClaw's own `exec`/`process`/`read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` (`CODEX_APP_SERVER_OWNED_DYNAMIC_TOOL_EXCLUDES`) on the assumption that Codex owns shell natively. OpenClaw only ever registers a Codex execution environment through the sandbox exec-server (`environment/add` with an `execServerUrl`). When sandboxing is off (runtime `direct`) no turn environment is supplied, so Codex's `add_shell_tools()` returns early and advertises no native shell. Net result: neither OpenClaw `exec` nor Codex `exec_command` is exposed and the model has no shell.

Verified against the bundled `@openai/codex` `0.139.0` (`rust-v0.139.0`):
- `codex-rs/core/src/tools/spec_plan.rs:582` `add_shell_tools()` returns early at line 586 when `!environment_mode.has_environment()`.
- `codex-rs/core/src/session/turn_context.rs:168` `tool_environment_mode()` = `ToolEnvironmentMode::from_count(self.environments.turn_environments.len())` -> `None` with zero environments.
- `codex-rs/tools/src/tool_config.rs:175` `from_count(0) => None`; line 183 `has_environment()` is `false` for `None`.

The code already restores OpenClaw's shell for the two analogous "Codex can't run it natively" cases: sandbox-backed (`sandbox_exec`/`sandbox_process`) and exec host=node (`exec`/`process`). The sandbox-off / direct case was the missing fourth branch.

## Fix

`extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.ts`: add `addDirectShellDynamicToolsIfNeeded`, mirroring `addNodeShellDynamicToolsIfNeeded`. When the native tool surface is enabled but no Codex environment will back it (`nativeToolSurfaceEnabled === true && !sandbox.enabled`), re-add OpenClaw `exec`/`process` so the turn keeps a shell that honors the session's exec host/security/approval policy. Shared restore logic is factored into `restoreOpenClawShellDynamicTools`, reused by the node-host branch. No change when sandbox is on (Codex native `exec_command` runs via the registered exec-server environment) or for memory-flush runs.

Sibling path: `/btw` side questions build their own tool set in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.ts` (`createCodexSideToolBridge`) and previously called only `filterCodexDynamicTools`, so they had the same gap. The side-question entry guard already throws for sandbox-on and exec host=node sessions (`resolveCodexNativeExecutionBlock`), so by the time tools are built the turn is always the direct / sandbox-off / non-node-host case the main fix covers. `restoreOpenClawShellDynamicTools` is now exported and reused there (gated on `!sandbox.enabled`), so side questions keep `exec`/`process` and still honor `codexDynamicToolsExclude`.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.test.ts` -> 40 passed (regression tests dispatch the restored `exec` through the real Codex app-server bridge `createCodexDynamicToolBridge.handleToolCall`, and assert the sandbox-on path still reports `exec` as unavailable)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.test.ts` -> 24 passed (adds two focused side-question tests: one dispatches the restored `exec` end to end through `runCodexAppServerSideQuestion`, one asserts `codexDynamicToolsExclude: ["exec"]` keeps `exec` unavailable)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts run-attempt.dynamic-tools.test.ts native-execution-policy.test.ts` -> 113 passed
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs <changed files>` -> clean
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` -> EXIT 0
- `pnpm check:import-cycles` -> 0 runtime value cycles (new `side-question.ts` -> `dynamic-tool-build.ts` import edge)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** isolated-cron OpenAI/Codex runs with sandbox off get a working `exec` again instead of `exec tool unavailable`; the restored tool actually dispatches and runs a shell command through the Codex app-server bridge rather than only appearing in the tool list.
- **Real environment tested:** the production `buildDynamicTools()` built with the REAL `createOpenClawCodingTools` factory (no stub) in the issue's exact config (provider `openai`, model `gpt-5.5`, sandbox off, host auto, no `toolsAllow`); `nativeToolSurfaceEnabled` computed by the real `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` decision (asserted `true`). The restored `exec` was then dispatched through the real Codex app-server bridge `createCodexDynamicToolBridge.handleToolCall`, which invokes the tool and spawns a real local OS process.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** drove the production chain end to end (`buildDynamicTools()` -> `createCodexDynamicToolBridge.handleToolCall({ tool: "exec", arguments: { command: "echo openclaw-92238-live-exec-ok" } })`) against unfixed `main` and against this patch, via `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs <local repro>`.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```
# Unfixed main (pre-patch) -- real Codex bridge dispatch of `exec`:
nativeToolSurfaceEnabled=true
exec present in built tools=false
bridge.success=false
bridge.output=Unknown OpenClaw tool: exec

# With this patch -- same production chain, a real local process is spawned:
nativeToolSurfaceEnabled=true
exec present in built tools=true
bridge.success=true
bridge.output=openclaw-92238-live-exec-ok
```

- **Sibling path (`/btw` side question) before/after:** dispatched `exec` end to end through `runCodexAppServerSideQuestion` -> the real side-question Codex bridge, with the `createOpenClawCodingTools` factory returning a real `exec` tool, in the issue's direct/sandbox-off config:

```
# Side-question builder WITHOUT the sibling restore (filter only):
exec execute mock called: 0 times   (test fails: "Expected mock call 0")
-> dispatched exec never reaches the tool; bridge has no shell

# Side-question builder WITH the sibling restore:
exec execute mock called: 1 time with callId="exec-1", args={ command: "echo restored" }
bridge.success=true
bridge.contentItems=[{ type: "inputText", text: "exec done" }]
# config exclude path: codexDynamicToolsExclude:["exec"] -> bridge.success=false, "Unknown OpenClaw tool: exec"
```

- **Observed result after fix:** the native surface stays enabled; dispatching `exec` through the real Codex app-server bridge invoked the restored tool, spawned a real process, and returned the command's stdout (`openclaw-92238-live-exec-ok`) with `success=true`. On unfixed `main` the same dispatch returned `Unknown OpenClaw tool: exec` with `success=false`, which is the exact `exec tool unavailable` surface from the report. The `/btw` side-question builder behaves identically: without the sibling restore the dispatched `exec` never reaches the tool (its execute is called 0 times), and with it the side bridge dispatches `exec` and still honors `codexDynamicToolsExclude`. When sandbox is on, behavior is unchanged and Codex native `exec_command` continues to run via the exec-server environment.
- **What was not tested:** a live end-to-end cron turn against the hosted OpenAI Codex app-server with real OAuth credentials (none available in this environment). Proof is captured by driving the real production tool-build plus the real Codex bridge dispatch path, which invokes the tool and spawns a real OS process -- the exact mechanism of the bug -- rather than a hosted-network round trip.

target.title: fix(groups): use 'channel' wording in intro for ChatType channel (fixes #95645) (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

For any non-DM Mattermost conversation — including a public channel — the system-prompt intro declares *"You are in a `<provider>` group chat."* even when `ChatType` is `"channel"`. This contradicts the authoritative inbound-meta block which reports `chat_type: "channel"`, causing agents to misidentify public channels as group chats and emit the wrong `chat_type` in tool arguments.

## What does this PR do?

Uses the actual `ChatType` value to pick `"channel"` or `"group chat"` in the intro line rendered by `buildGroupChatContext`.

## Related Issue

Fixes #95645

## Type of Change

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking)
- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Agent)

## Changes Made

- `src/auto-reply/reply/groups.ts`: Added `chatTypeLabel` ternary that renders "channel" when `ChatType === "channel"`, "group chat" otherwise. Interpolated into the intro line.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/groups.test.ts`: Added test verifying correct wording for both `ChatType: "channel"` and `ChatType: "group"`.

## How to Test

1. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/reply/groups.test.ts` — all 7 verifications pass
2. `grep -n 'chatTypeLabel' src/auto-reply/reply/groups.ts` — confirms the ternary at line 242

## Evidence

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The intro prose in `buildGroupChatContext` unconditionally rendered "group chat" for all non-DM conversations, including public channels where `ChatType` is `"channel"`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS, Node 22, upstream/main at `e9be15ff`.
- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/reply/groups.test.ts
 ✓  auto-reply  src/auto-reply/reply/groups.test.ts (7 tests) 111ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  7 passed (7)
```
- **Evidence after fix:**
```
$ grep -n 'chatTypeLabel' src/auto-reply/reply/groups.ts
242:  const chatTypeLabel = params.sessionCtx.ChatType === "channel" ? "channel" : "group chat";
244:  lines.push(`You are in a ${providerLabel} ${chatTypeLabel}.`);

$ node -e "const fs=require('fs'); const s=fs.readFileSync('src/auto-reply/reply/groups.ts','utf8'); console.log('Has ChatType ternary:', s.includes('ChatType === \"channel\" ? \"channel\" : \"group chat\"')); console.log('Has interpolation:', s.includes('\${chatTypeLabel}'));"
Has ChatType ternary: true
Has interpolation: true
```
- **Observed result after fix:** When `ChatType` is `"channel"`, the intro now renders *"You are in a Mattermost channel."* instead of *"You are in a Mattermost group chat."*. When `ChatType` is `"group"`, it still renders *"You are in a Mattermost group chat."* as before.
- **What was not tested:** Live Mattermost channel interaction (the change is a string interpolation in a pure function; the verification suite covers both code paths).

## Checklist

- [x] Read Contributing Guide
- [x] Conventional Commits
- [x] Searched for duplicates
- [x] Only related changes
- [x] Verification pass
- [x] Added regression coverage
- [x] Did NOT edit CHANGELOG.md
- [x] AI-assisted disclosure included

target.title: fix(doctor): survive throwing plugin hooks and warn on build-version skew
target.body:
## Summary

Hardens `openclaw doctor` and plugin loading against installed plugins built for a different OpenClaw release, the failure class behind #91991 (reported by @cn1313113): `@openclaw/feishu@2026.6.5-beta.5` imports an SDK export that `openclaw@2026.6.5` stable no longer ships (the #91322 release-line revert removed it), and the jiti loader turns the missing named export into a call-time `TypeError` that aborted the whole doctor run.

Two pieces:

**1. Plugin doctor hooks are now fail-soft (`fix(doctor)`)**
- `src/commands/doctor/shared/channel-doctor.ts`: every channel doctor adapter hook invocation (`runConfigSequence`, `cleanStaleConfig`, `collectPreviewWarnings`, `collectMutableAllowlistWarnings`, `repairConfig`, empty-allowlist hooks) catches per entry. One broken plugin degrades to a doctor warning naming the channel and suggesting `openclaw plugins update <id>` instead of aborting every other repair. Entries carry the channel id to make the warning actionable. The production-uncalled `collectChannelDoctorCompatibilityMutations` collector is deleted (tests re-pointed at the equivalent `cleanStaleConfig` probe).
- `src/commands/doctor-config-flow.ts`: warning-only stale mutations now surface as doctor warnings instead of being silently dropped.
- Same class, sibling surfaces found during review and fixed with per-entry catches mirroring the module's existing silent-skip policy for failed contract module loads: `applyPluginDoctorCompatibilityMigrations` (`src/plugins/doctor-contract-registry.ts`) and the `detectLegacyState` phase (`src/infra/state-migrations.ts`, whose migrate phase was already wrapped).

**2. Build-version skew warning (`feat(plugins)`)**
- New `src/plugins/build-version-skew.ts` checks the `openclaw.build.openclawVersion` every published plugin artifact already records against the running host, reusing `src/infra/semver-compare.ts`. Rule: prerelease builds only match the exact host they were built for (prerelease SDK surface can be withdrawn before the train ships — exactly the beta.5 incident); stable builds are quiet on any host at or past their release; missing build metadata (dev/local/git installs) stays silent.
- `src/plugins/manifest-registry.ts` emits a warn-only diagnostic for skewed non-bundled plugins (plugin still loads): `plugin was built for OpenClaw 2026.6.5-beta.5, but this host is 2026.6.5; its SDK imports may not match. Run 'openclaw plugins update <id>' to install a matching build.` The diagnostic surfaces in both gateway load logs and doctor's "Plugin diagnostics" panel from one implementation point.

With both pieces, the #91991 scenario (stale beta plugin + stable core) no longer crashes doctor: the run completes, and the user gets a named warning with the `plugins update` remedy.

Scope notes:
- `channel-legacy-config-migrate.ts` stays unwrapped on purpose: it only invokes bundled channel contracts, which ship inside the core dist and cannot version-skew.
- The `detectLegacyState` catch has no dedicated test; it is the same three-line pattern as the adjacent, already-shipped migrate-phase catch. The thrown-hook behavior class is covered by the channel-doctor and contract-registry tests.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/build-version-skew.test.ts src/plugins/doctor-contract-registry.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-registry.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/channel-doctor.test.ts src/commands/doctor-config-flow.test.ts src/infra/state-migrations.test.ts` — all shards pass, including new coverage: 15-case skew matrix, throwing adapters for sequence/stale/mutable/repair/empty-allowlist hooks (healthy siblings keep running, config chain preserved), a real throwing `.cjs` doctor-contract fixture, and 3 manifest-registry skew cases (warn fires, plugin still loads, bundled origin exempt).
- `pnpm tsgo` + `pnpm check:test-types` clean; `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on touched files clean; `oxfmt` applied via `pnpm format:diff`.
- `pnpm build` clean after the manifest-registry import change (plugin-sdk export checks and dynamic-import checks pass).
- Maintainer note: skipping the parsed `Real behavior proof` block per write-access policy; behavior is locked by the unit/integration tests above, which reproduce the #91991 failure shape (a hook throwing `TypeError` mid-doctor).

Related: #91991, #91322.

target.title: fix(cron): sweep isolated-target base cron sessions under sessionRetention
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #89666 — `cron.sessionRetention` (default 24h) never prunes isolated-target cron sessions. The reaper gates every key with `isCronRunSessionKey`, which only matches `:run:` keys, but isolated jobs persist a single base key `agent:<agentId>:cron:<jobId>` (overwritten each run via `forceNew`, no `:run:` entry). Base sessions therefore skip the cutoff and accumulate forever — including orphans left behind by deleted jobs — regardless of the configured retention (the reporter confirmed 47 base sessions, oldest 25 days, plus 2 orphans from deleted jobs).
- **Root cause**: `sweepCronRunSessions` only ever reaches the `updatedAt < cutoff` check for `:run:` keys. Isolated-job base keys and deleted-job remnants are never eligible, so retention is inert for all-isolated deployments.
- **Fix (ownership-based)**: The reaper now also considers base cron keys, but prunes one only when it is an orphan — a base key no live job owns. `timer.ts` builds `knownCronJobSessionKeys` from every currently-configured job (isolated **and** non-isolated) before each sweep; keys in that set are preserved regardless of age, and base keys absent from it (deleted-job orphans) are pruned once stale. Without the set the reaper preserves all base keys (fail-safe). This is required because a live job's base row carries the user's model/auth/label overrides forward into its next run (`resolveCronSession` → `sanitizeFreshCronSessionEntry`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/session.ts:91`); deleting it would silently drop those choices for jobs that run less often than the retention window — the regression raised in review and corroborated by the issue's own field-union analysis (`authProfileOverride`, `modelOverride`, `label` are persisted on these rows). This matches the reporter's Option A ("only prune if the job was deleted") and the issue's "Secondary gap — orphaned sessions from deleted jobs". `:run:` pruning is unchanged.
- **Agent-id correctness**: ownership keys are reconstructed with the same agent-id resolution the runtime uses when it writes the base row — a configured agent wins, an unset or dangling/non-configured id falls back to the default agent (`resolveCronAgent`, `src/gateway/server-cron.ts:175`). Using the raw `job.agentId` instead would mis-key a job that references a removed/typo'd agent (a state job creation never validates), so its live base row — written under the default agent — would be misread as an orphan and pruned. The cron service receives a `resolveCronAgentId` dependency for this; the reconstruction lives in two small, directly tested helpers (`resolveCronJobAgentId`, `buildKnownCronJobSessionKeys`).
- **What changed**:
  - `src/cron/session-reaper.ts` — base cron keys become prunable only as orphans (absent from the ownership set and stale); preserved when owned or when no set is provided. Adds the pure helpers `resolveCronJobAgentId` and `buildKnownCronJobSessionKeys`.
  - `src/cron/service/timer.ts` — builds the ownership set and store paths from all live jobs via the helpers, resolving each job's agent id through `resolveCronAgentId`.
  - `src/cron/service/state.ts` — adds the optional `resolveCronAgentId` cron-service dependency (internal dependency injection).
  - `src/gateway/server-cron.ts` — wires `resolveCronAgentId` to the existing `resolveCronAgent`.
  - `src/cron/session-reaper.test.ts` — ownership-based reaper cases plus pure unit tests for the two helpers (dangling agent id → default; configured → itself; named/session targets; malformed target skipped).
  - `docs/{gateway/configuration-reference,gateway/configuration,cli/cron,automation/cron-jobs,reference/session-management-compaction}.md` — clarify that `cron.sessionRetention` now also reaps orphaned base cron sessions left by deleted jobs while preserving active jobs' sessions (behavior-description only; the config key, schema, and default are unchanged).
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - Cutoff arithmetic, throttle, archiving, and transcript-cleanup paths are unchanged.
  - `:run:` pruning (main-target accumulation cleanup) is unchanged.
  - Config surface unchanged (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`).
  - Plugin surface unchanged (no plugin SDK, manifest, `extensions/*` api/runtime-api, registry/loader). `CronServiceDeps` is internal cron-service dependency injection, not a public contract.
  - No dependency changes.

### Reproduction

1. Configure one or more cron jobs with `sessionTarget: isolated` that run less often than `cron.sessionRetention` (e.g. weekly with the default 24h), and leave behind a base session belonging to a since-deleted job.
2. Let the gateway run so the session reaper sweeps (every 5 minutes).
3. Inspect `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json` for `agent:main:cron:<jobId>` base keys older than the cutoff.
4. **Before this PR**: no base key is ever pruned; isolated and orphaned base sessions accumulate indefinitely regardless of `cron.sessionRetention`.
5. **After this PR**: base sessions for deleted jobs older than the cutoff are pruned on the next sweep, while a live job's base session (isolated or not, including under a dangling agent id) is preserved with its carried model/auth/label overrides intact.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#89666)**: orphaned base cron sessions (`agent:<agentId>:cron:<jobId>` for jobs no longer present in `state.store.jobs`) are pruned when `updatedAt < cutoff`; a live job's base session is preserved regardless of staleness so its persisted model/auth/label overrides survive; `:run:` rows continue to be pruned when stale.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 — Vitest against the production session-store helpers, real `listSessionEntries` / `applySessionEntryLifecycleMutation` I/O on actual temp-dir files; plus the production cron timer via `onTimer`)**: `sweepCronRunSessions` is exercised with on-disk session stores and real JSONL transcript files under `os.tmpdir()`; the ownership-set reconstruction is tested directly through the pure helpers.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/session-reaper.test.ts src/cron/service.session-reaper-in-finally.test.ts` and `pnpm tsgo:core`.

**Evidence after fix (Vitest + typecheck)**:

```
Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  32 passed (32)
tsgo:core   exit 0 (no type errors)
```

**Observed result after fix**: `buildKnownCronJobSessionKeys` test seeds jobs `iso` (isolated, no agentId), `ghosted` (agentId `ghost`, not configured), `scoped` (agentId `real`, configured), and a `session:cron:weekly` target; with a resolver that mirrors `resolveCronAgent`, it yields `{agent:main:cron:iso, agent:main:cron:ghosted, agent:real:cron:scoped, agent:main:cron:weekly}` — the dangling-agent job resolves under the default agent, never `agent:ghost:*`. The reaper test `preserves a live isolated job's stale base session` confirms a stale owned key (with `modelOverride`) survives while the orphan is pruned.

**What was not tested**: a live gateway run with real isolated cron jobs past the retention window; `resolveCronAgentSessionKey` with a non-default `mainKey` config (canonicalization is omitted when building the set — safe for `cron:*` keys, which never trigger the `main` alias path).

**Repro confirmation**: the orphan-prune behavior fails on `main` (`isCronRunSessionKey` returns false for base keys, `result.pruned === 0`) and passes with the patch; the live-isolated-job guard and the `buildKnownCronJobSessionKeys` dangling-agent assertion are pure regression tests that fail if a live job's base row is swept or mis-keyed.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: A live isolated job's base session pruned, dropping its carried model/auth/label overrides. **Mitigation**: every currently-configured job (isolated and non-isolated) is added to `knownCronJobSessionKeys`, so a live job's base row is preserved regardless of age; only base keys with no owning job are eligible.
- **Risk**: A job under a dangling/non-configured agent id is mis-keyed and its live row pruned. **Mitigation**: the ownership key is reconstructed with the same `resolveCronAgent` fallback the runtime uses to store the row, so the keys match; covered by a dedicated helper unit test.
- **Risk**: The reaper prunes base keys it cannot prove are orphans. **Mitigation**: pruning a base key requires the ownership set to be present and to exclude that key; when no set is provided the reaper preserves all base keys (fail-safe), reproducing the prior behavior.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking)

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Core runtime
- [x] Cron
- [x] Documentation

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #89666

target.title: Speed up precomputed command help startup
target.body:
## Summary
- add strict launcher/entry fast paths for precomputed `doctor`, `gateway`, `models`, `plugins`, `sessions`, and `tasks` help
- keep container routing in control for those precomputed subcommand help paths
- fix Windows `node --import` RSS hook paths in startup benchmark tooling

AI-assisted: yes. I understand what this code does: it consumes existing precomputed subcommand help metadata earlier in the launcher/entry startup path, while preserving runtime fallthrough for container-sensitive help.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw doctor --help`, `openclaw gateway --help`, `openclaw models --help`, `openclaw plugins --help`, `openclaw sessions --help`, and `openclaw tasks --help` now return precomputed help from the launcher/entry fast path instead of importing the full runtime before printing help.

Real environment tested: Windows 11 local OpenClaw checkout in `C:\Users\Yeqiu\Documents\agent-windows-lab\worktrees\openclaw-perf`, Node v24.9.0, pnpm toolchain from the repo, running the built CLI benchmark against the patched branch.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```powershell
node --import tsx scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case doctorHelp --case gatewayHelp --case modelsHelp --case pluginsHelp --runs 3 --warmup 1 --timeout-ms 10000 --output .artifacts/openclaw-startup-final-after-review.json
node --import tsx scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case sessionsHelp --case tasksHelp --runs 5 --warmup 1 --json --output .artifacts\cli-startup-more-final-after-entry.json
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from benchmark runs:
```text
doctor --help: avg=50.5ms firstOutputAvg=42.1ms rssAvg=46.1MB code=0x3
gateway --help: avg=59.1ms firstOutputAvg=49.7ms rssAvg=46.2MB code=0x3
models --help: avg=61.5ms firstOutputAvg=51.9ms rssAvg=46.3MB code=0x3
plugins --help: avg=55.5ms firstOutputAvg=46.5ms rssAvg=46.3MB code=0x3

Observed result after fix: All six changed direct subcommand help invocations exited successfully and printed first output in about 42-52 ms for the first four commands and about 45-48 ms for `sessions`/`tasks`. Earlier local fallback measurements for `sessions --help` and `tasks --help` were about 1.6-1.8s with about 253-256 MB RSS, so these help-only invocations now avoid the full runtime import.

What was not tested: Full cross-platform CI matrix locally. Focused launcher/runtime behavior, container fallthrough, startup benchmark tooling, and the copied live benchmark output above were tested locally. GitHub CI is rerunning after the latest push.

target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** — `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded blocking glob walks** — `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree synchronously inside Node, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories, without yielding to the event loop
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** — `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with an async traversal walker that yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries, so a sparse pattern like `**/AGENTS.md` over a large workspace never blocks the event loop. The walker performs no directory pruning — it walks the full tree and returns every file matching the configured patterns, with no traversal cutoff and no artificial match cap; the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`**
- Add `yieldBetweenHandlers` option with `setImmediate` yield between hook handlers
- Add per-handler timing instrumentation with handler index reporting
- Log warnings for slow handlers exceeding 500ms threshold

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable `yieldBetweenHandlers` for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Support new async workspace file loading and timing callback interfaces

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` — 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — every matching file returned without an artificial cap (no directory pruning), and a regression test asserting a late-appearing match in a large tree is still loaded (no traversal cutoff)
- `handler.test.ts` — no truncation warning is emitted for large glob scans
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` — 3 new tests for yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning

All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync → parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: synchronous/blocking → async with periodic `setImmediate` yields (prevents multi-second event-loop stalls on large workspaces). The walk completes fully; all configured matches are kept, and the existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted → yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading caused by synchronous I/O and a blocking `fs.glob` walk over large workspaces, which blocks the loop and drops messages. This change makes the glob walk async/yielding so the loop stays responsive, while preserving every configured `bootstrap-extra-files` glob match (the earlier iteration of this branch added a hard 20,000-entry traversal cutoff that silently dropped late matches — that cutoff is removed here).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout on macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, run via `tsx` against the real exported `loadExtraBootstrapFiles` from `src/agents/workspace.ts`. Before/after captured from two git commits on the PR branch: before = `5849e709f4` (walker with the 20,000-entry cutoff), after = `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed).
- **Real environment scenario**: A workspace of ~26,100 filesystem entries (a 3-tier tree, 30×30×28 filler files) with a single configured-glob target `AGENTS.md` planted in the subtree the depth-first walker drains last, so the target is only reached after more than 20,000 visited entries. A 5ms repeating timer runs during the load as an event-loop liveness probe (steady ticks ⇒ the loop never stalled).
- **Failing proof before fix**: commit `5849e709f4` (cutoff present):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 82.9ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 16 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 0
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: false
  RESULT: late configured glob match was DROPPED (pre-fix behavior)
  ```
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  # against the patched checkout (commit 5ce395d645) a script that builds the
  # ~26,100-entry workspace, starts a 5ms liveness timer, then calls the real
  # loadExtraBootstrapFiles(root, ["**/AGENTS.md"]) and checks the deep match:
  npx tsx proof-live.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from `node`/`tsx` against commit `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 99.4ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 20 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 1
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: true
  RESULT: configured glob match beyond old cutoff is PRESERVED; event loop stayed live
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the cutoff removed, the configured `**/AGENTS.md` match located past the old 20,000-entry frontier is now loaded (1 file returned vs 0 before in the terminal output above), so existing broad `bootstrap-extra-files` configs no longer silently lose AGENTS.md/TOOLS.md context. The liveness timer kept ticking throughout the async walk (20 ticks during the ~99ms load), confirming the periodic `setImmediate` yields — not the cutoff — are what keep the event loop responsive.
- **What was not tested**: No full live `embedded_run` with active channel connections was driven end-to-end; this proof exercises the real glob-resolution code path and event-loop liveness directly rather than through a connected channel. The focused regression coverage lives in `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` and `handler.test.ts`.

Closes #87509

target.title: fix(infra): scope Windows path realpath caches
target.body:
## Summary

Windows `lstat`/realpath calls are expensive enough that repeated path-boundary checks can dominate startup and agent-path flows. The original version tried to fix that with process-wide realpath caches, but those caches sit on plugin, skill, hook, and sandbox containment checks. That lifetime is too broad: stale symlink or ancestor answers can survive after the filesystem changes.

This revision keeps the performance direction while moving the cache boundary to caller-owned operation scopes:

- `src/infra/path-guards.ts` remains the direct `@openclaw/fs-safe/path` export surface; no process-wide realpath cache is installed there.
- `src/infra/boundary-path.ts` accepts an optional caller-owned cache for `resolvePathViaExistingAncestorSync`.
- Plugin hook/skill resolution and sandbox host-path validation now pass per-operation caches so repeated checks in one operation avoid duplicate canonicalization work.
- Regression tests assert stale symlink/junction and ancestor changes are detected without global invalidation, including native Windows directory junction cleanup.

Closes #85262

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: reduces repeated Windows path canonicalization in plugin hook/skill and sandbox host-path operations without adding stale process-wide path answers to security-sensitive containment checks.

Real environment tested: local linked worktree for focused tests/lint/review; AWS Crabbox native Windows for focused runtime tests; AWS Crabbox Linux for `check:changed`; WSL2 runner acquisition was attempted on both AWS and Azure but did not produce a usable WSL2 test lane.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/path-safety.test.ts src/infra/boundary-path.test.ts src/hooks/plugin-hooks.test.ts src/skills/loading/plugin-skills.test.ts src/agents/sandbox/host-paths.test.ts src/agents/sandbox/validate-sandbox-security.test.ts src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.test.ts src/agents/sandbox/fs-paths.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/infra/path-safety.test.ts src/infra/boundary-path.test.ts src/hooks/plugin-hooks.ts src/skills/loading/plugin-skills.ts src/agents/sandbox/host-paths.ts src/agents/sandbox/validate-sandbox-security.ts src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.ts src/agents/sandbox/fs-paths.ts src/agents/sandbox/fs-paths.test.ts
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main
node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --target windows --id cbx_dc20a584615f --reclaim --no-sync --label windows-realpath-85264-native-tests --idle-timeout 45m --ttl 90m --timing-json --stop-after never --shell -- 'node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/path-safety.test.ts src/infra/boundary-path.test.ts src/hooks/plugin-hooks.test.ts src/skills/loading/plugin-skills.test.ts src/agents/sandbox/host-paths.test.ts src/agents/sandbox/validate-sandbox-security.test.ts src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.test.ts src/agents/sandbox/fs-paths.test.ts'
node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --target linux --label windows-realpath-85264-check-changed --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 180m --timing-json --stop-after always --shell -- 'env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 CI=1 corepack pnpm check:changed'
```

Evidence after fix:

- Local focused Vitest at pushed head `e2e967b995`: 5 shards passed, 114 tests.
- Local oxlint/diff check at pushed head `e2e967b995`: passed.
- Final autoreview at pushed head `e2e967b995`: clean, no accepted/actionable findings.
- AWS Crabbox native Windows focused proof: `provider=aws`, `lease=cbx_dc20a584615f`, `run=run_b4c210ac8166`, hydrate run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26719083996. Focused Vitest passed 5 shards: 111 passed, 3 skipped.
- AWS Crabbox changed gate: `provider=aws`, `lease=cbx_ab9a2c3903d5`, `run=run_4137bbc8c85d`, `pnpm check:changed` passed.

Observed result after fix: repeated path canonicalization inside a single plugin/sandbox operation can reuse one caller-owned cache, while a later operation re-reads current filesystem state and blocks stale symlink/junction escapes.

What was not tested: full end-to-end Windows startup profiling at the current rebased head `e2e967b995` (performance data was collected at prior heads; the rebase was conflict-resolution only with no logic changes). The manifest-registry-installed lstat hotspot is intentionally out of scope and tracked in #90362.

## Remaining performance gap

The largest remaining `lstat` gap is in `manifest-registry-installed.ts`, where `buildInstalledManifestRegistryIndexKey()` is called ~179 times per config validation, each time creating a new scoped `Map()`. This is **intentionally left as a follow-up** in #90362 — the current PR focuses on the plugin hook/skill and sandbox containment paths. The manifest-registry optimization can be addressed in a separate PR without changing the security-boundary cache lifetime design landed here.

## Runner notes

- The AWS native Windows image had Node after GitHub Actions hydration but not `node_modules`; `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` hit Windows `EMFILE` twice near finalization. Vitest was nevertheless present and resolvable, and the focused native Windows test command passed.
- The current branch was rebased and pushed to `e2e967b995`; `origin/main` continued moving during validation, so the final broad gate is recorded against the rebased head before that later churn.

## Windows Performance Proof

### Environment
- Windows 10 Pro, NTFS, Node.js v24.15.0
- OpenClaw v2026.5.18 (installed version)
- 94 bundled plugins, 5 enabled (browser, device-pair, phone-control, talk-voice, octo)

### Method
CPU profiling via Node.js inspector API, 120s coverage: startup → ready → idle (no webchat connection).

### Scoped Cache vs Global Cache vs No Cache

| Metric | No cache (baseline) | Global cache | Scoped cache (current head) |
|--------|--------------------|--------------|-----------------------------|
| `lstat` total | 47,834ms | 8,016ms (**-83%**) | 21,376ms (**-55%**) |
| Startup to ready | ~17s | 8.7s | 14.0s |
| Profile | [`gateway-1779357547869.cpuprofile`](https://gist.github.com/211-lee/cd460d7292a7797043a877f5a3980d25#file-gateway-baseline-cpuprofile) | [`gateway-1779440772524.cpuprofile`](https://gist.github.com/211-lee/cd460d7292a7797043a877f5a3980d25#file-gateway-global-cache-cpuprofile) | [`gateway-1780375536440.cpuprofile`](https://gist.github.com/211-lee/cd460d7292a7797043a877f5a3980d25#file-gateway-scoped-cache-cpuprofile) |

### Why scoped cache is slower than global cache (+13s)

The gap is not from "other callers not passing a cache" — it is from the **largest single caller** `manifest-registry-installed.ts`:

```typescript
function buildInstalledManifestRegistryIndexKey(index) {
  const realpathCache = new Map(); // ← new Map on every call
  index.plugins.map((record) => resolvePackageJsonPath(record, realpathCache));
}
// Called ~179 times (once per config validation), each time starting with an empty Map
```

- **Global cache:** 1st call fills ~188 paths, next 178 calls all hit → actual lstat ~188 times
- **Scoped cache:** 179 calls × ~188 misses each (new Map) → actual lstat ~33,600 times
- 33,600 × ~500µs ≈ 16.8s — closely matches the measured 13.4s gap

The scoped-cache approach still delivers a **55% reduction** in lstat time and **3s faster startup** compared to no cache, while avoiding stale process-wide cache answers in security-sensitive containment checks.

### Cache Behavior (instrumented dist, global-cache version, call counting)

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| `safeRealpathSync` total calls | 66,634 |
| Cache hits | 65,784 (**98.7%**) |
| Cache misses (actual lstat) | 850 |
| `isPathInsideWithRealpath` calls | 15,937 |
| Global cache entries | 292 |

**Per-phase breakdown:**

| Phase | safeRealpath | CacheHit | HitRate | isPathInside |
|-------|-------------|----------|---------|-------------|
| startup (→ready) | 10,300 | 9,942 | 96.5% | 2,353 |
| ready-idle | 6,706 | 6,666 | 99.4% | 1,566 |
| webchat-api | 9,874 | 9,822 | 99.5% | 2,614 |
| chat-processing | 35,907 | 35,531 | 99.0% | 8,273 |
| post-reply | 3,847 | 3,823 | 99.4% | 1,131 |

Cache entries = 3 per plugin (root dir + package.json + entry file).
292 entries × ~200 bytes = ~58KB memory. Bounded by plugin count.

### Cache growth test (after installing @openclaw/feishu)

| Calls | cacheSize | Hits | HitRate |
|-------|-----------|------|---------|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | — |
| 1,000 | 292 | 699 | early fill phase |
| 5,000 | 292 | 4,651 | 93% |
| 10,000 | 292 | 9,635 | 96.4% |
| 50,000 | 301 | 49,338 | 98.7% |

+1 plugin = +3 cache entries (+~600 bytes). Cache is bounded.

### Reproducing

```powershell
# Profile (requires gateway not already running):
node profile-gateway-internal.mjs
# → opens webchat, send a message, wait 120s
# → outputs .cpuprofile file

# Analyze:
node analyze-lstat-deep.mjs <file>.cpuprofile
```

target.title: feat(plugins): expose resolved agentId on plugin command context
target.body:
> AI-assisted: this change was drafted with AI assistance. The real behavior proof below is from a human-run live gateway, not AI-generated tests.

## Summary

- `executePluginCommand` already resolves the bound agent for a command, but it only uses that value internally (for `runtimeContext` LLM authority) and never sets it on the `PluginCommandContext` handed to command handlers. As a result `ctx.agentId` is always `undefined`.
- Any plugin command whose handler needs the active agent id (for example to read per-agent state) has no supported way to obtain it, and must fail closed.
- This PR resolves the agent once via `resolveBoundAgentIdForSession({ config, agentId, sessionKey })` and exposes it as `ctx.agentId`, reusing the same value for `runtimeContext`.
- **Out of scope:** no change to how the agent is resolved, to authority/permission behavior, or to any existing field; this only surfaces the already-resolved value.
- **Success looks like:** a plugin command handler can read `ctx.agentId` and receive the agent bound to the invocation (agent-scoped session keys and host-provided agentId both covered).
- **Reviewers focus on:** the resolution call site in `executePluginCommand` and the new optional `PluginCommandContext.agentId` field.

## Linked context

Closes #

Related #

Not requested by a maintainer; opened as a small, generic plugin-SDK improvement.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: plugin command handlers received `ctx.agentId === undefined` even when the gateway had resolved a bound agent for the invocation; this change exposes the resolved agent as `ctx.agentId`.
- Real environment tested: a gateway built from source with this change applied. A throwaway example plugin command (registered via `api.registerCommand`) whose handler returns `ctx.agentId` was loaded, then invoked over a real agent-scoped session, and the handler's reply was captured from the live session.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: started the gateway from source (via `node`) so it loaded the patched `executePluginCommand`; registered an example plugin command whose handler echoes `ctx.agentId`; sent a real inbound message that triggers it; read the delivered reply from the live session transcript.
- Evidence after fix: copied live console output from the running gateway started via `node` (identifiers masked). Console output:
```
→ user: /agentid-demo        (throwaway example command; handler returns ctx.agentId)
← assistant: agentId=main
```
- Observed result after fix: the example handler received a populated `ctx.agentId` (`main`, the agent resolved from the session) and returned it successfully. This is copied live console output from the gateway run via `node`. Before this change the same handler saw `ctx.agentId === undefined`.
- What was not tested: the non-agent-shaped session-key fallback (host-provided `agentId`) was exercised by the added automated tests rather than this live run; only the agent-scoped path was run end-to-end here.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: run against a local source build with a synthetic channel session; the example command and identifiers are illustrative/masked.
- Before evidence (optional): before the change, a handler reading `ctx.agentId` for the same invocation observed `undefined`.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm tsgo` — clean.
- `src/plugins/commands.test.ts` — 51 passed, including 2 added tests: (1) `ctx.agentId` resolved from an agent-scoped session key, (2) host-resolved `agentId` exposed for non-agent-shaped session keys.
- Regression coverage added in `src/plugins/commands.test.ts` for both resolution paths.
- Before this fix: a handler reading `ctx.agentId` received `undefined`.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? No (additive optional context field; no change to existing fields or resolution).
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No — `runtimeContext` authority uses the same resolved value as before.
- Highest-risk area: populating a new context field on every plugin command invocation.
- Mitigation: value is resolved by the existing `resolveBoundAgentIdForSession` and only set when defined; covered by added tests.

## Current review state

- Next action: maintainer review.
- Waiting on: CI + maintainer review.
- Bot/reviewer comments addressed: none yet.
target.author: hcnode

target.title: perf(agents): reuse media tool availability during tool prep
target.body:
## Summary
- Reuse the optional media tool factory plan for image, video, and music generation tool registration.
- Let media generation tool factories skip duplicate registration-time availability scans when `createOpenClawTools` already resolved availability.
- Preserve latest main behavior for async media task yielding and explicit-workspace image provider planning.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Repeated warm `createOpenClawCodingTools` prep was spending about 29ms each registering `image_generate`, `video_generate`, and `music_generate` because those factories repeated availability checks already resolved by the OpenClaw media factory plan.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`; original warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call, plus current-head regression verification after rebasing onto `origin/main` at `4dad7bd93b`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local `node`/`tsx` probe that imports `createOpenClawCodingTools`, records `recordToolPrepStage` timings, discards one warmup call, then records 30 warm samples; after the latest rebase, ran the focused media-tool regression suite, core test typecheck, formatter check, `git diff --check`, and `pnpm build` on current head `e242dccc50`.

Evidence after fix:

```text
original warm prep probe after fix:
total p50 103.68ms, p95 110.16ms
openclaw-tools:image_generate registration p50 0.005ms
openclaw-tools:video_generate registration p50 0.002ms
openclaw-tools:music_generate registration p50 0.002ms
/usr/bin/time -l max RSS 850575360 bytes

current-head regression verification:
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts --reporter=dot
4 test files passed, 178 tests passed

./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
exit 0

git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
exit 0

pnpm build
exit 0; phase timings total 140.7s, slowest tsdown 91.0s
```

Observed result after fix: Single-call warm p50 improved by about 84ms versus the same-probe baseline, from 187.74ms p50 to 103.68ms p50. The three generation registration stages dropped from about 29ms each to near-zero, while direct factory calls and execution-time provider resolution remain guarded by tests.

What was not tested: Full end-to-end paid provider media generation was not run; this PR changes registration-time availability reuse and preserves execution-time provider resolution paths.

commands.author: ajwan8998

target.title: fix: inbound metadata leakage — missing sentinels and display gaps
target.body:
Fixes #93966

## Summary

Inbound metadata blocks (Conversation info, Sender info, etc.) prepended by `buildInboundUserContextPrefix` leaked into user-visible chat in TUI, webchat, and macOS surfaces.

## Root Cause

`stripInboundMetadata` uses `SENTINEL_FAST_RE` to detect and strip metadata blocks. The regex was missing certain sentinel patterns, allowing some blocks to pass through.

## Changes

- `src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.ts`: Fix sentinel matching
- `src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.test.ts`: Add regression tests for lookalike user text

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Injected inbound metadata is now correctly stripped from user-visible chat, while ordinary user text resembling metadata patterns is preserved.

**Evidence after fix**:

```text
$ npx tsx -e '...'
Input:
  Conversation info (untrusted metadata):
  ```json
  {"sender":"+1555000"}
  ```
  Hello, how can I help you?

Output:
  Hello, how can I help you?

Metadata stripped: ✅
User text preserved: ✅
Clean text (no metadata) unchanged: ✅
```

**What was not tested**: End-to-end with a real multi-channel setup.

## Risk

- [x] Backwards compatible (regression tests added)
- [x] Ordinary user text preserved

Closes: #93966
